title: E2E live-loop test mode: daemon-backed PTY test proving event-driven redraw
description:
Add a daemon-aware mode to the PTY harness (tests/shell_pty.rs) and a first end-to-end test proving the live prompt redraws on a chevrond event. Today every PTY test sets CHEVRON_NO_DAEMON=1 and no daemon runs, so the event->redraw path (the differentiator) has zero e2e coverage; only string-assertion unit tests exist (shell.rs:2314-2405).

Approach: reuse the in-process daemon spawn from src/daemon/client.rs tests (state::spawn(TTL, db) + listener::serve_loop on a hermetic socket under the test XDG_RUNTIME_DIR). Drive zsh with CHEVRON_LIVE=1 pointed at that socket, inject a state change (FS event in a watched repo, or a CMD_END), assert via the vt100 grid that the prompt re-rendered. Verify-it-bites: must fail if the live spawn block (shell.rs:689-692) or callback is removed.

Precondition for flipping CHEVRON_LIVE on by default, and the direct answer to rubric finding chevron-vz7 (boldest path, least tested).
notes: Prototype delivered on branch prototype/live-e2e-ffu1 (uncommitted). Added tests/shell_pty.rs::live_prompt_redraws_on_daemon_event + git_in helper: in-process chevrond on hermetic socket; $HOME git repo on branch base; zsh with CHEVRON_LIVE=1 + CHEVRON_SOCKET_DIR + NO_DAEMON unset; switches branch to livebranch on disk and injects StateMsg::FsEvent with ZERO keystrokes; asserts prompt repaints to new branch (proves subscribe->zle -F->async render->reset-prompt). Passes 7/7 locally, clippy clean. #[ignore]'d pending CI/multi-terminal shake-out (= this epic's acceptance). Harness mode now reusable for ffu.2/ic3/eim/1mh. FINDING: CLAUDE.md Known-gaps note that init does not load zsh/datetime is STALE -- shell.rs:626 loads EPOCHREALTIME.

title: Graduate live prompt (CHEVRON_LIVE) from dark-launch to default-on
description:
The reactive "live prompt" -- the prompt redraws between keystrokes on chevrond events (FS-watched git changes, commands finishing in other panes) -- is chevron's most structurally-differentiated capability. starship is stateless; powerlevel10k refreshes git only at precmd; atuin has no prompt. Nobody else can redraw a stock-terminal prompt on a cross-session event.

The zsh implementation SHIPPED in chevron-1yn.3 (commit aba79c5) but is GATED OFF by default (CHEVRON_LIVE=1), per the note at src/shell.rs:686: "Off by default until shaken out across terminals."

This epic is the finish line: make the live prompt trustworthy, robust, and cross-shell, then flip the default so the differentiation is actually visible to users.

## Why it's the keystone
Every reactive frontier feature -- cross-pane awareness (chevron-i1h), per-cwd memory hints (chevron-a05), output-aware segments -- depends on the prompt being able to redraw on a daemon event. The capability exists; it is invisible because it is dark-launched and unverified end-to-end.

## Current gaps (verified in src/shell.rs, 2026-06-11)
- No e2e coverage. Only string-assertion unit tests (shell.rs:2314-2405); the PTY harness runs CHEVRON_NO_DAEMON=1 and never starts a daemon, so event -> redraw is unverified. This is THE blocker to "shaken out" and the rubric's central finding (chevron-vz7): the boldest path is the least-tested.
- No auto-reconnect (chevron-1mh): on EOF (daemon idle-timeout / restart / crash) the callback unsets the fd and the live prompt silently dies for the rest of the session (shell.rs:657-661).
- No subscription scoping: spawns `chevron subscribe` with no cwd filter (shell.rs:690), so every shell wakes on every repo's events. Relate chevron-409 (topic filtering at the daemon).
- bash (chevron-eim) and fish (chevron-ic3) live unimplemented.
- Hardening unverified: the live callback fires BETWEEN prompts and reuses the async pipeline whose stale-guard bumps generation only in precmd. Confirm correctness in the accept-window, PS2-continuation, and interrupt cases.

## Definition of done
CHEVRON_LIVE defaults to on, backed by: e2e tests that bite; reconnect across daemon restarts; cwd-scoped subscriptions; and at least zsh + one other shell. Then the differentiating demo (a prompt that updates on its own) is real for every user, not a gated flag.

title: lookback-tool survey: port applicable edge cases from p10k, ble.sh, pure, oh-my-posh, starship
description: Cloned seven lookback-modifying prompt tools to /tmp/prompt-research and mined their edge-case handling vs chevron (four parallel reviews; full delta in session notes). Implemented in this pass: (1) rewrite gated on the collapse having actually painted -- alternate accept widgets (accept-and-hold, custom .accept-line callers) used to get the full prompt half-erased and overpainted; (2) exact-margin width skip -- transient width % COLUMNS == 0 ends in auto-margin pending-wrap, emulators disagree on the next LF advance (ble.sh xenl trap), saved row off by one painted duplicated chevrons (reproduced in harness); (3) async callback no longer zle reset-prompt during PS2 continuations (pure's CONTEXT==cont guard); (4) fish Enter gating ported from starship/OMP: commandline --is-valid / empty-buffer check, --paging-mode skip, fish_cancel + postexec flag clear, vi insert/visual mode bindings.
close_reason: Implemented the four applicable gaps: collapse-flag gating (alternate accept widgets), exact-margin xenl skip, PS2 reset-prompt guard, fish validity/pager gating + cancel/postexec flag clear + vi-mode bindings. PTY regression tests alternate_accept_widget_skips_rewrite and exact_width_input_skips_rewrite_cleanly verified to fail pre-fix; unit assertions for the PS2 guard and fish gating. Deferred follow-ups filed separately (transient-on-interrupt polish, VTE geometry race). Clones remain in /tmp/prompt-research.

title: ^C during DSR exchange/sweep leaves terminal raw with echo off
description: No always-block guards the stty dances: SIGINT during zselect/read/drain in _chevron_query_row or the precmd sweep unwinds the hook chain mid-function, skipping the stty restore and fd close. Terminal left raw -echo min 0 time 0; the NEXT exchange saves the wedged state via stty -g and faithfully re-restores it, making the wedge permanent until reset. The linger fix (chevron-6gd) widened the window to a guaranteed 300ms drain on timed-out cycles; Silent-DSR terminals pay 300ms preexec + 300ms sweep per command. Fix: wrap both tty dances in zsh { try } always { restore } blocks (the powerlevel10k pattern); always-lists run on interrupt and on early return.
close_reason: Fixed: both tty dances (query helper, precmd sweep) wrapped in { try } always { restore } blocks; always-list runs on interrupt and on early return. Regression test ctrl_c_during_dsr_sweep_leaves_tty_sane (Silent DSR, ^C landed 100ms into the sweep drain, stty -g state compared before/after) verified to fail 3x pre-fix. Known cosmetic residue: an interrupted precmd serves the collapsed stub as the next prompt for one cycle.

title: DSR response leaks as kernel-echoed ^[[row;1R around interactive reads (read -rs)
description: Observed: read -rs "ASHBY_API_KEY?Paste Ashby write key: " left literal ^[[68;1R on screen followed by the 12.7s duration tag. The caret form means KERNEL echo: the response arrived while the tty was cooked with echo on and nobody reading. Two holes: (1) precmd's rewrite query has no sweep behind it -- preexec's timeout is swept at the next precmd, but when the REWRITE query itself times out (response >300ms or displaced by junk) the straggler lands between stty restore and the next ZLE session and gets kernel-echoed at the cursor, exactly where the duration tag then prints; (2) an interactive read builtin consuming/being raced by an in-flight response can corrupt what it reads (secret keys!). Fix direction: precmd's query must drain inline on timeout while still raw (it has no later sweep point), i.e. a linger mode on _chevron_query_row; flip raw before the zselect probe so pending partial-line input is visible (cooked-mode select only reports complete lines); related: chevron-cuo.
close_reason: Fixed: _chevron_query_row takes a linger window; precmd's rewrite query passes 30 (300ms) so a timed-out response is drained inside the helper's raw window instead of kernel-echoing after echo restore. PTY regression test slow_precmd_dsr_response_never_leaks (new Dsr::AlternateDelayed + Render::SyncDelayed harness modes) verified to fail pre-fix 3x.

title: bare exec in fd dance permanently redirected shell stderr to /dev/null
description: A bare zsh exec persists every redirection on it, so the query helper's 'exec {fd}< /dev/tty 2>/dev/null' (e875615) pointed the whole shell's stderr at /dev/null from the first prompt cycle: all command error output vanished and ncurses reset/tset exited 1 silently (they reach the terminal via fd 2) -- surfaced as 'the reset command no longer works'. Same class in the precmd sweep (93a93e7), instant-prompt takeover/zshexit restores (d29ce27, trailing 2>/dev/null was the LAST redirection on fd 2 so it applied unconditionally), and the live-callback close (aba79c5). Fixed in d2d51fa: brace-group scoping for allocated-fd opens, suppression dropped from fd-2 restores. Regression tests command_stderr_stays_visible + reset_command_reinitializes_cleanly (PTY, verified to fail pre-fix) plus a unit assertion.
close_reason: Fixed and pushed in d2d51fa on unstable; brace-group scoping at all five sites, PTY regression tests verified to bite pre-fix.

title: Shell preamble breaks when user has alias :='cd ..'
description:
The chevron-igl preamble emits `: "\${VAR:=value}"` for each shell-config default. The `:` is the POSIX null command — fine in most setups, but a common dotfile pattern aliases `:` (and `::`, `:::`) to navigate up directories:

  alias :='cd ..'
  alias ::='cd ../..'

When the preamble runs in a shell with this alias, each `:` line expands to `cd .. "\${VAR:=1}"`. zsh's `cd OLD NEW` form then tries to substitute `..` in PWD (which never contains literal `..`), producing:

  (eval):cd:2: string not in pwd: ..
  (eval):cd:3: string not in pwd: ..
  ... (one per preamble line)

The CHEVRON_* vars don't get set, so the body's `${CHEVRON_X:-N}` patterns fall back to their hardcoded defaults. Functionally users see the defaults, just with an alarming error block at every shell startup.

## Fix

Replace `: "${VAR:=value}"` with direct assignment: `export VAR="${VAR-value}"`. Semantically equivalent (assigns default when unset) but doesn't invoke the `:` builtin, so no alias collision. Also exports for consistency with the env-var-as-override model.

## Test

Add a unit test asserting the preamble does not start lines with `:` (so this regression can't reappear).

## Effort

XS (~10 LoC change in shell.rs preamble generators, test updates).
close_reason: Fixed: preamble now uses `export VAR="${VAR-default}"` direct assignment instead of `: "${VAR:=default}"`. The colon-builtin form was alias-expanded by zsh when users had `alias :='cd ..'` defined (common dotfile pattern), producing 6 `string not in pwd: ..` errors at every shell startup. Direct assignment bypasses the alias entirely. Added regression test that the preamble does not start any line with `: `. Body unchanged. 621 lib tests pass.

title: Local rename: Cargo, source, shell init, flake, scripts, docs
description:
The in-tree mechanical changes:

- Cargo.toml: name = 'chevron', repository URL → shiprock/chevron, add dual [[bin]] entries (chevron primary, plx alias). Both point to src/main.rs.
- src/main.rs, benches/segments.rs: 'use plx::' → 'use chevron::'. Verify cargo check + cargo bench --no-run.
- src/shell.rs: init_zsh/init_bash/init_fish output 'chevron <cmd>' calls instead of 'plx <cmd>'. Snapshot tests will catch any miss.
- flake.nix: pname = 'chevron', add postInstall symlink $out/bin/plx → chevron for back-compat. Verify nix build still works.
- scripts/bench.sh: BIN=./target/release/chevron.
- README.md: full rewrite, add 'formerly plx' note, install instructions show 'cargo install chevron' and 'nix run github:shiprock/chevron'.
- CLAUDE.md: rewrite project description, file paths still accurate, mention chevron throughout.
- AGENTS.md: rewrite.
- Health check / weather error messages that say 'plx:' → 'chevron:'.

Acceptance: full test suite passes (lib + integration + benches build), 'plx' compat binary exists and produces identical output to 'chevron', release build runs cleanly.

Does NOT touch GitHub or crates.io — those are separate sub-tasks.

title: GitHub: transfer mmichie/plx to shiprock org, rename to chevron
description:
One-time admin operation via gh CLI (or GitHub web UI):

1. Push any pending work first (ensure local master is clean).
2. Transfer mmichie/plx → shiprock org: 'gh repo transfer mmichie/plx shiprock' (or web UI). Requires confirmation from a shiprock admin.
3. Once transferred, rename: 'gh repo rename --repo shiprock/plx chevron'.
4. GitHub serves 301 redirects from mmichie/plx → shiprock/chevron for both repo URL and git remote.
5. Update local git remote: 'git remote set-url origin git@github.com:shiprock/chevron.git'.
6. Verify: 'gh repo view shiprock/chevron', confirm Actions/issues/PRs migrated.
7. Update Cargo.toml repository URL to the new path (done in plx-7rc.1 if not already).

Done by user — requires shiprock org admin perms and confirmation prompts. Cannot be fully automated by agent.
close_reason: transfer + rename complete: mmichie/plx → shiprock/plx → shiprock/chevron. Local remote updated to git@github.com:shiprock/chevron.git, fetch verified, HEAD matches origin/master at ca88e129. GitHub serves 301 redirects from the old URL so any existing clones keep working.

title: Rebrand plx → chevron, transfer mmichie/plx → shiprock/chevron
description:
First public release under a new name. plx is taken on crates.io (Samuel Roland's learning tool). chevron is available everywhere and is semantically tied to powerline arrows. Move repo to shiprock org as the umbrella brand.

Constraints:
- Binary name 'plx' MUST stay shipped as a compat alias (back-compat for existing users like Pat). The new primary binary is 'chevron'; both point to the same source via dual [[bin]] entries.
- Shell init (src/shell.rs) emits 'chevron' command calls going forward, but old configs running 'plx' still work because the alias binary exists.
- crates.io publish is a one-way door (you can yank but not unpublish) — be sure on the name before first publish.

Order: this must complete BEFORE plxd phase 1 (plx-1yz.1) so the daemon ships as chevrond, not plxd.

See sub-tasks for the breakdown.
close_reason: Published chevron 0.5.0 to crates.io and tagged v0.5.0 (pushed). Nix flake confirmed working via 'nix run github:shiprock/chevron'. Co-owner add deferred: requires creating a 'shiprock/<team>' team on GitHub first.

title: plxd phase 0: extract RepoStatus from segments::git::render
description:
Pull the data-computation half of segments::git::render into a pure RepoStatus struct + compute(repo) function. Render becomes pure formatting over RepoStatus.

This is the load-bearing refactor for the plxd daemon: it gives the daemon a stable data type to cache and serve, and lets the inline-fallback and daemon paths share identical compute code (the invariant that makes daemon-as-pure-optimization safe).

Independent value beyond the daemon: enables snapshot-style render tests over hand-built RepoStatus values, the way tmux_title::render_from_info works today. Cheaper, more precise, no tempdir+libgit2 per test.

Scope (~200 LoC change):
- New src/repo_status_data.rs (final name TBD — current repo_status.rs is the interactive status view; rename one) holding RepoStatus + compute()
- segments::git::render(status, from_bg) becomes pure formatting; remove the libgit2 calls from render
- Update callers in segments::git::render (top-level) and segments::tmux_title to call compute() then render()
- Reuse GitInfo::gather logic (it already does this split for tmux-title); generalize to cover all fields
- Add snapshot tests for render over fixed RepoStatus inputs

Must not regress the no-daemon perf path — verify with the criterion benches added in plx-32m.
close_reason: phase 0 complete: RepoStatus extracted from segments::git::render; 283 lib tests + 42 integration tests passing; perf neutral-to-improved (render_clean 690→468µs, render_20_untracked 664→548µs, compute_cwd unchanged at ~640µs); snapshot tests cover all 7 canonical render scenarios

title: Fold tmux title into plx prompt
description: Generate tmux title inside plx prompt as second output line when $TMUX is set. Eliminates redundant Repository::discover and reduces tmux IPC calls from 5-6 to 1-2, saving ~36ms per prompt.
close_reason: Implemented two-line output protocol, all 64 tests pass, clippy clean

title: PS2 live-event biting regression test (CONTEXT==cont guard)
description:
Biting regression test for invariant (b) from the ffu.2 audit: a daemon event firing during a PS2 continuation (CONTEXT==cont) must not corrupt secondary-prompt state. The live callback inherits the [[ $CONTEXT != cont ]] && zle reset-prompt guard (shell.rs:570).

Design (from ffu.2 audit): use the daemon-backed harness from chevron-ffu.1 (extract a shared LiveFixture). Enter a PS2 continuation (e.g. unterminated quote), fire FsEvents while in cont, then COMPLETE the multiline and assert the command produced correct output and the shell recovered to a clean prompt. Do NOT assert mere absence of the new branch during PS2 -- that does not bite (with the guard removed, reset-prompt repaints PS2, not PS1). Bite-verify per CLAUDE.md: delete shell.rs:570, confirm the completion misbehaves, restore.

title: Live: cwd-scoped subscription + chpwd re-subscribe (configurable for cross-pane)
description: The live spawn opens 'chevron subscribe' with no cwd filter (shell.rs:690), so every shell redraws on every repo's FS/cmd events -- a thundering herd with many repos/shells. Scope the subscription to the shell cwd, and add a chpwd hook to re-subscribe on directory change (no chpwd hook exists today). Relate chevron-409 (topic filtering at the daemon): decide client-side cwd filter vs daemon-side topic filter. Keep it configurable -- the cross-pane feature (chevron-i1h) WANTS other-pane events, so scope must be opt-out-able.

title: Flip CHEVRON_LIVE default to on + README + configure wizard option
description: Flip CHEVRON_LIVE default from 0 to 1 (shell.rs preamble + ShellConfig.live default), document the live prompt in README, and surface it in 'chevron configure'. GATED: only after the e2e tests bite, auto-reconnect (chevron-1mh) lands, and the hardening audit is green. This is the step that makes the differentiator visible to every user instead of a hidden flag.

title: Live-loop hardening audit: between-prompt invariants (accept-window, PS2, interrupt, debounce)
description: Audit and test the live callback when it fires BETWEEN prompts. It reuses _chevron_start_async, whose stale-guard compares _chevron_async_spawn_gen to _chevron_async_gen, and the generation bumps only in precmd (shell.rs:478,548). A live-driven spawn does not bump the generation, so confirm: (a) a live redraw landing in the accept window is dropped by the collapse guard (shell.rs:556); (b) a live redraw during PS2 continuation does not reset-prompt (CONTEXT==cont, shell.rs:570); (c) ^C during the live callback or its async child leaves no raw-mode / fd leak; (d) two events within KEYTIMEOUT do not double-spawn (debounce, shell.rs:670-675). Each case needs a regression test (depends on the e2e harness mode).
design:
Live-loop between-prompt invariant audit (2026-06-12).

The live callback (_chevron_live_callback, shell.rs:651) reuses _chevron_start_async -> _chevron_async_callback, inheriting their guards. Findings per case:

(a) ACCEPT-WINDOW -- NOT REACHABLE by the live path. zle -F handlers run only while ZLE is active. From accept-line (_chevron_transient_collapsed=1, shell.rs:580) through command execution to precmd (which clears the flag, shell.rs:436), ZLE is inactive, so the live callback cannot fire during the collapse window. The shared collapse guard (shell.rs:556) still protects the async-refresh path from landing there (chevron-6tc). No live-specific risk; no new test required.

(b) PS2 CONTINUATION -- REACHABLE; the one real gap. During a multiline/continuation edit CONTEXT==cont and ZLE is active, so a daemon event DOES fire the live callback. It is guarded by `[[ $CONTEXT != cont ]] && zle reset-prompt` (shell.rs:570), inherited from async_callback (pure/p10k guard identically). Needs a regression test -- spun out as chevron-ffu.2.1.
  CAUTION for the test author: the failure mode is secondary-prompt STATE corruption, not PS1 content bleeding onto the screen. A naive "assert the new branch does not appear during PS2" does NOT bite, because with the guard removed `zle reset-prompt` repaints PS2, not PS1. A biting test must assert cont-state integrity: enter PS2, fire events, COMPLETE the multiline, and assert the command produced correct output and the shell recovered to a clean prompt; bite-verify by deleting shell.rs:570 and confirming the completion misbehaves.

(c) INTERRUPT (^C) -- LOW RISK. Unlike the DSR tty-dances, the live/async callback performs no stty/raw-mode changes, so there is no raw-mode-leak exposure. The only residue is a narrow fd-leak window if SIGINT lands between `exec {fd}<` and `zle -F` in _chevron_start_async; the fd is process-substitution-scoped and reaped on the next cycle. Optional trap/always-block hardening is not warranted by the severity. No test required now.

(d) DEBOUNCE DOUBLE-SPAWN -- ALREADY COVERED by the unit test zsh_live_callback_debounces_event_bursts (shell.rs:2360). No e2e needed.

CONCLUSION: 3 of 4 cases require no new work (unreachable / low-risk / already-covered). The single concrete remaining task is the PS2 biting regression test (chevron-ffu.2.1), designed above. The daemon-backed harness from chevron-ffu.1 (tests/shell_pty.rs) is the vehicle; extract a shared LiveFixture when implementing it.
notes: Audit complete (2026-06-12): 3/4 between-prompt cases need no new test (a unreachable via inactive ZLE; c low-risk no-stty; d already unit-covered at shell.rs:2360). Only the PS2 biting regression test remains -> chevron-ffu.2.1.

title: Expose chevrond as a stable session-query API (daemon-as-platform)
description:
chevrond already speaks a versioned line protocol over a Unix domain socket (src/daemon/proto.rs: HELLO / STATUS / CMD_START / CMD_END / SUBSCRIBE / VERSION / QUIT) and persists a structured session record (commands.db: cmd, cwd, exit, duration, session_id, hostname, output_bytes). Today only chevron's own prompt and CLI consume it.

This decision proposes treating that socket + protocol as a FIRST-CLASS, STABLE, DOCUMENTED API that other tools (editors, tmux, status bars, scripts) can query: "what did I run in this repo today", "what failed on this branch", "what is running in other panes", "stream me state changes for cwd X".

## Why this is the biggest moat-widener
chevron's defensible position (per rubric chevron-vz7) is the unified session substrate that competitors keep in separate tools. A stable query API reframes chevron from "a prompt" into "the local session-state service that other tools build on" -- a platform position no incumbent holds: starship is stateless, atuin's DB is private to atuin, gitstatusd is git-only. The prompt becomes one client of the substrate, not the product.

## What it unlocks
- Third-party prompt / statusbar integrations (tmux status-right, editor status lines) reading session state without re-deriving it.
- A scriptable `chevron query` interface (jq-friendly JSON) over the session DB and the live event stream.
- The frontier features (cross-pane, per-cwd memory, output-diff) become API consumers instead of bespoke shell code.

## What we are deciding
Whether to commit to API stability for a documented subset of the protocol (a read-only QUERY verb + the SUBSCRIBE stream, versioned via the existing HELLO/VERSION handshake), versus keeping the protocol an internal implementation detail.

Status: direction-setting ADR. No commitment to implement yet. Implementation is gated behind (1) live-prompt graduation -- the SUBSCRIBE half of the API must be trustworthy first -- and (2) history-CLI maturity (chevron-3eq).
design:
Design notes and rationale.

## Wire format
Add a read-only QUERY verb returning JSON; keep the existing line protocol for STATUS/CMD_*/SUBSCRIBE. Version it through the existing HELLO/VERSION handshake so clients negotiate. Rejected: a full RPC framework (gRPC) -- over-engineered for a local per-user UDS.

## Stability contract
Pin a documented, stable subset of fields. Lean on the schema-readiness already done in chevron-1yn: ULIDs, ms timestamps, hostname + session_id first-class. Mark everything else explicitly unstable.

## Security / privacy (load-bearing)
The socket is per-user under XDG_RUNTIME_DIR; document the trust boundary as local-user-only. Output capture (Phase 4) makes this a privacy surface: never expose captured output bytes over the API without redaction, and gate output access behind explicit opt-in. Tie to chevron-ckz (secret detection / redaction). This must ship WITH any query-over-output capability, not after.

## Alternatives considered
(a) Keep the protocol internal; expose only via `chevron history` CLI. Rejected long-term: forces every integrator to shell out and parse human-formatted output, and offers no live stream.
(b) Full gRPC/RPC service. Rejected: heavy dependency and operational surface for what a local UDS + JSON QUERY verb already covers.
(c) Do nothing. The substrate stays a private implementation detail and the platform moat is never realized -- the single largest strategic opportunity in the rubric goes unspent.

## Relationship to other work
- Local-only by construction. Federated sync (chevron-wvn / chevron-n1x) is a separate axis and out of scope here.
- The SUBSCRIBE stream is half of this API; its trustworthiness is the live-prompt graduation epic. Do not publish a "stable" SUBSCRIBE contract until that epic's e2e tests are green.

## Open questions
- Exact QUERY surface: free-form query language vs. a fixed set of named queries. Lean fixed + named first (smaller stability surface).
- Discovery: how a third-party tool finds the socket and version. Document the XDG path + HELLO handshake.

title: Evaluation rubric for chevron (weighted, innovation-led)
description:
Weighted evaluation rubric for chevron. Scale 1-5 per dimension (1 Rudimentary, 3 Solid, 5 Exemplary). Weights sum to 100; innovation weighted highest by request.

## Weights
- Innovation in the space: 30
- Correctness: 20
- Well-testedness: 15
- Ease of use: 13
- Extensibility: 12
- Ease of development: 10

Peer set the rubric judges against: starship, powerlevel10k/gitstatusd, oh-my-posh, pure, ble.sh, atuin.

## Innovation (30) -- 5 facets, averaged
- 1a Novelty: does something incumbents do not, AND holds a combination none of them holds.
- 1b Technical depth on hard problems: terminal-protocol races, DSR/KEYTIMEOUT splits, auto-margin/xenl wrap, tty-dance interrupt safety.
- 1c Robustness of the innovation (not a demo): novel mechanisms bounded against hostile inputs and proven under simulated adversity (Dsr responder modes, render latency).
- 1d Transferable methodology: ideas the ecosystem could adopt -- vt100 + configurable-DSR-responder PTY harness; no-panic render contract enforced by lint.
- 1e Problem reframing / scope ambition: reconceiving the prompt as the front-end of a shell-session daemon, disciplined not sprawling. Highest ceiling AND highest risk facet.

## Correctness (20)
Adversarial correctness. Each known failure mode guarded AND locked by a regression test verified to fail pre-fix; non-panic enforced by lint (prompt renders every keystroke; unwrap/expect/panic forbidden in prod, freed in cfg(test)); external failure paths degrade silently and safely (weather exits 0 empty; git2 not subprocess; async generation-stamp discards superseded refreshes).

## Well-testedness (15)
Multi-paradigm (unit / property / snapshot / CLI / PTY-integration / bench) + adversarial harness simulating a hostile terminal + regression tests proven to bite + CI flake detection (5x). Docked for documented coverage gaps: PTY harness zsh-only; bash/fish transient + OSC 133 untested e2e; daemon + git segment have no e2e coverage (all PTY tests run CHEVRON_NO_DAEMON=1 outside a repo).

## Ease of use (13)
One-line init across zsh/bash/fish; zero-config defaults (weather needs no API key); interactive `configure` wizard with presets + live preview; safe failure paths that never corrupt the terminal; clean interop (starship custom modules, tmux status-right).

## Extensibility (12)
Segment registry + [segments]/[segment.<name>] config toggles + custom_command escape hatch + provider trait (weather) + feature flags. Ceiling: single static binary (new segment = recompile, same as starship); per-shell init hand-ported across three shell.rs dialects; no runtime/plugin loading.

## Ease of development (10)
nix reproducibility; lefthook gates (fmt + pedantic clippy; pre-push 5x tests + nix build); criterion benches; CLAUDE.md encodes shell.rs invariants AND the verify-it-bites procedure. Friction: shell.rs irreducibly subtle (2409 lines, 90 tests, the documented "most subtle code"); PTY harness needs zsh (skips in nix sandbox, so nix build cannot catch shell regressions); unstable/master divergence is a standing hazard.

## Snapshot (first-pass self-assessment, 2026-06-11 -- a starting line, not authoritative)
- Innovation 4.6 -> 27.6 (facets: 1a 5, 1b 5, 1c 4, 1d 5, 1e 4.5)
- Correctness 5.0 -> 20.0
- Well-testedness 4.2 -> 12.6
- Ease of use 4.7 -> 12.2
- Extensibility 3.8 -> 9.1
- Ease of development 4.2 -> 8.4
- TOTAL ~= 89.9 / 100

Two findings the rubric surfaces: (1) the boldest work (daemon, git segment, bash/fish transient) is also the least e2e-tested -- the innovation gap and the coverage gap are the same place. (2) The scope reframe (facet 1e) is the biggest asset and the biggest liability; it warrants an explicit "prompt vs session-daemon" identity decision.
design:
Rationale for the weighting.

Why innovation = 30 (highest): the user named innovation as most important. chevron competes in a crowded space (starship / powerlevel10k / oh-my-posh) where parity alone is not a reason to exist. The defensible position is the unified daemon substrate -- prompt + command history + live FS-watched state + pub/sub -- that no incumbent combines. The rubric must reward that substrate over raw feature count.

Why correctness = 20 (second): the prompt re-renders on every keystroke. A panic drops the user's prompt; a leaked DSR response self-inserts into the command line. Correctness is existential here in a way it is not for a stateless CLI, so it outweighs testedness and every UX/dev dimension. It is a gate, not a moat.

Why parity is NOT scored as innovation: language-runtime segments, RPROMPT, OS icon, battery/memory/disk segments (epics chevron-8u6, chevron-3eq) are table stakes versus starship/atuin. They earn ease-of-use and adoption but contribute ~nothing to the innovation axis. Innovation credit is reserved for capabilities that REQUIRE chevron's unified substrate and that competitors structurally cannot copy: per-cwd/per-branch memory surfaced in the prompt, live event-driven redraw between keystrokes, cross-pane/cross-session awareness, output-diff across runs. Epic chevron-qo5 is the innovation locus; chevron-8u6 and chevron-3eq are parity.

Alternatives considered:
(a) Equal weights -- rejected; ignores the user's stated priority and the competitive reality.
(b) Correctness highest -- rejected; correctness is necessary but, at chevron's current maturity, it is a gate, not the differentiator.
(c) Fold testedness into correctness -- rejected; the documented e2e gaps are a distinct, separately-actionable signal that deserves its own axis.

Supersede or update this record if the weighting changes or the snapshot is re-scored.

title: env-knob contracts untested: CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0 / CHEVRON_OSC133=0
description: CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0 is the documented escape hatch for terminals where the DSR machinery misbehaves, and CHEVRON_OSC133=0 the opt-out for OSC-hostile terminals — but nothing asserts either actually silences its emissions. A regression here strands exactly the users who opted out. Add raw-stream PTY assertions: zero ESC[6n bytes with transient off (and zero collapse/chevron glyphs), zero ESC]133 bytes with OSC off.
close_reason: transient_disabled_sends_no_dsr_queries (zero ESC[6n bytes, zero chevron glyphs) and osc133_disabled_emits_no_markers (zero ESC]133 bytes) pin both escape hatches at the raw-stream level.

title: instant-prompt snippet has no end-to-end PTY coverage
description: The paste-in top-of-zshrc snippet (chevron-nf8) paints a cached prompt, captures .zshrc stdout/stderr to a buffer via exec redirection, and hands over in precmd (fd restore, line clear, buffer replay). It owned one of the five stderr-nuking exec bugs (chevron-9ah) and has zero PTY-harness coverage because the hermetic .zshrc never includes the snippet. Add a harness variant that seeds the cache file, writes snippet+init to .zshrc, and asserts: cached paint occurred (raw stream), buffered .zshrc output replayed, real prompt takes over, and stderr is functional afterwards.
close_reason: instant_prompt_paints_takes_over_and_restores_fds: seeds the cache, installs the snippet at the top of the hermetic .zshrc, asserts cached paint (raw stream), .zshrc output replay at takeover, and functional stderr afterwards. Built on a new spawn_customized fixture hook.

title: DSR exchange truncated by typed R drops typeahead and strands the response
description: In _chevron_query_row, input that lands between the zselect probe and the read and contains a capital R (e.g. 'echo DR...') terminates read -d R BEFORE the DSR response arrives. The buffer then contains no CSI, so the print -z re-inject branch never fires: everything typed up to the R is eaten silently, and the real response stays queued (observed leaking as literal ^[[4;1R in the PTY probe). Fix: when the read buffer has no CSI, treat it all as typeahead (re-inject printable content) and flag the response in flight so the sweep gets it; also note the zselect probe runs on a cooked tty and cannot see pending PARTIAL lines (canonical mode reports readability at line boundaries), so raw mode should flip before the probe.
close_reason: Fixed: raw mode now precedes the zselect probe (partial-line typeahead visible, query skipped); CSI-less read buffers are re-injected via print -z with the eaten R restored. PTY regression test paste_leftovers_during_interactive_read_stay_clean verified to fail pre-fix.

title: Move user-facing shell-init toggles from env vars to config
description:
Close the gap: segment config is already TOML-driven (Config::load read by PromptContext::gather), but the six shell-init toggles bypass that path and live as env vars only. Config.toml becomes the single source of truth; env vars remain as runtime overrides (env > config > default — atuin/starship convention).

## Inventory & classification (audit complete)

**MOVE to config (this issue):**
- CHEVRON_OSC133, CHEVRON_TRANSIENT, CHEVRON_TRANSIENT_DURATION_MS, CHEVRON_ASYNC, CHEVRON_HISTORY, CHEVRON_LIVE → new `[shell]` block
- CHEVRON_REPO_RELATIVE_PATH → `[segment.path].repo_relative`

**STAY env-only:**
- CHEVRON_CONFIG (bootstraps the file path — chicken-and-egg)
- CHEVRON_SOCKET_DIR / CHEVRON_LOG_DIR / CHEVRON_HEALTH_CACHE (system paths)
- CHEVRON_SHELL / CHEVRON_CACHE_FILE / CHEVRON_SESSION_ID (init↔prompt plumbing pipes)
- CHEVRON_NO_DAEMON / CHEVRON_CAPTURE_MAX_MB (admin escape hatches)
- CHEVRON_WEATHER_* (already overlay the [weather] block correctly)

## Mechanism

`chevron init <shell>` becomes parameterized on `&ShellConfig`. Today it returns `&'static str`; after refactor it returns `String` with a generated preamble prepended to the (unchanged) body:

```bash
# Generated from ~/.config/chevron/config.toml [shell]
: "${CHEVRON_OSC133:=1}"
: "${CHEVRON_TRANSIENT:=1}"
: "${CHEVRON_TRANSIENT_DURATION_MS:=2000}"
: "${CHEVRON_ASYNC:=0}"
: "${CHEVRON_HISTORY:=1}"
: "${CHEVRON_LIVE:=0}"

<existing 360-line init body, unchanged>
```

The `${VAR:=value}` idiom sets `VAR` only if unset. So env > config > default falls out naturally. Body's inline `${VAR:-DEFAULT}` checks become belt-and-suspenders (still work if init is sourced without preamble).

Fish syntax: `set -q VAR; or set -gx VAR value`.

## Implementation order

1. **Config schema**: add `ShellConfig` struct + `Config.shell` field. Six fields with defaults matching today. ~40 LoC.
2. **Shell init refactor**: `init_zsh(&ShellConfig) -> String`, same for bash/fish. Body → `const BODY: &str`. Generated preamble per shell. ~80 LoC.
3. **main.rs dispatch**: `chevron init <shell>` loads Config, passes `&cfg.shell`. ~10 LoC.
4. **shell.rs test updates**: ~40 tests need to pass `&ShellConfig::default()`. Body assertions still pass (body unchanged). Add new tests asserting preamble reflects config values. ~150 LoC churn.
5. **Move CHEVRON_REPO_RELATIVE_PATH**: add `repo_relative` field to `SegmentConfig` (or new path-specific block). Update path segment to read config first, env override second. ~30 LoC.
6. **Wizard collapse**: `configure::emit::to_config_toml` writes `[shell]` block; `to_shell_snippet` becomes a single `eval "$(chevron init zsh)"` line. ~50 LoC churn.
7. **Doctor legacy detection**: scan user's .zshrc/.bashrc/fish-config for `export CHEVRON_*` lines that overlap with the new `[shell]` block. Emit `[info]` row: "These can move to ~/.config/chevron/config.toml". Non-fatal. ~50 LoC.

## Acceptance

- `chevron init zsh` output starts with the generated preamble, body unchanged
- Setting `[shell].transient = false` in config + no env override → transient disabled in next shell
- `export CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=1` in user's rc OVERRIDES config (env > config)
- Wizard `--dry-run` shows a `[shell]` block in TOML and a single `eval` line in the snippet
- `chevron doctor` flags legacy `export CHEVRON_*` lines as info-level migration hints
- All existing shell.rs tests still pass (body unchanged)
- New tests verify preamble reflects config (`config{transient: false}` → preamble has `:=0`)

## Tradeoffs

1. Init cost: each shell startup reads+parses TOML. ~2-5ms cold. Acceptable; measure with criterion.
2. Malformed config: reuses existing `Config::load` warn-and-default path — broken TOML never breaks init.
3. Belt-and-suspenders: body retains `${VAR:-DEFAULT}` patterns, redundant in normal path. Kept for safety if init is sourced raw.

## Effort

~410 LoC + ~150 LoC test churn = ~560 LoC. Single PR.

## v2 follow-ups (not this issue)

- Per-shell preamble cost measurement + caching (if 2-5ms is meaningful)
- Audit if any segment env vars beyond REPO_RELATIVE_PATH are user-facing and should move
close_reason: Shipped: [shell] block in config.toml is now the source of truth for the six init toggles, with env-var override precedence (env > config > default) via the shell value idiom. CHEVRON_REPO_RELATIVE_PATH moved to [segment.path].repo_relative. Wizard snippet collapsed to single eval line. Doctor flags legacy env exports for migration. 685 tests pass.

title: Per-cwd memory hint on chpwd
description:
When the user `cd`s into a directory chevrond has seen before, emit a one-line recap of what they did there last time. "Restores context for context-switching humans" — the killer first-impression UX.

## Why this is frontier

No competitor has this because they don't have:
- Persistent daemon (for instant lookup, not 200ms cold SQL)
- Native git awareness (to include branch state in the recap)
- chpwd hook integration (chevrond's Phase 3 push channel is the right shape)

Atuin can do "history --cwd ." manually. p10k/Starship don't even know about history. Nobody else turns "cd into project-X" into a free context-restoration UI.

## Surface

- chpwd hook in shell init: emits CHEVRON_CWD_CHANGED event to chevrond
- chevrond reads commands + commands_selections tables for this cwd, computes a recap
- Recap pushed back to shell via subscribe channel; shell prints it as a single subtle line before the next prompt:

  ↳ 3 days ago in ~/project-X: 12 commands, 2 failed; last branch: feature/y; last `cargo build`: passed in 38s

- Configurable via `[memory_hint]` config section:
  - enabled (bool, default off until users opt-in)
  - max_age_days (default 14 — don't surface ancient context)
  - format (template-based, similar to chevron history format strings)

## Acceptance criteria

- chpwd to a known cwd prints the recap line within ~50ms
- chpwd to a never-seen cwd prints nothing (silent)
- Disabled by default (CHEVRON_MEMORY_HINT=0 / config opt-in)
- chpwd to a cwd seen >14 days ago prints nothing (or a different "Last seen N days ago: ..." variant)
- Snapshot-tested with fixture sessions

## Effort

M (~400 LoC across shell init + daemon recap query + push). Reuses Phase 3 subscribe channel.

## Synergy

- Phase 3 subscribe is the push channel for chpwd-triggered recap (or even reuse it as the transport: shell calls `chevron history --recap-cwd $PWD` instead, simpler)
- Existing aggregation work for chevron-niv (stats subcommand) provides recap-data shape
- Branch context (chevron-1ys.1.1, sibling): if available, surface "last branch: feature/y" in the recap

## Sequence

After branch + commit context (the recap line is much richer with branch info) but not strictly blocked.

## Out of scope

- "Restore session" — actually setting environment to mirror last visit. Just informational hint, no state restoration.
- Per-machine vs cross-machine recap. v1 is just THIS machine; cross-machine comes with sync.
- LLM-summarized recap. Hand-rolled template format only.

## What this looks like in practice

A user comes back to a project after vacation:

  $ cd ~/work/important-thing
  ↳ Last here 2 weeks ago: 47 commands across 3 sessions
  ↳ Branch: feature/migration (now 12 commits behind origin)
  ↳ Last 3 cmds: `cargo test --release`, `git push`, `git status`
  $ _

The whole point is that this restores 5 minutes of "where was I?" in 50ms.

title: Branch + commit context on every history row
description:
Add `branch` and `head_sha` columns to the commands table. Populate at CmdStart time using libgit2 (chevrond already linked).

## Why this is frontier

Atuin, mcfly, p10k, Starship — none of them store git context per command. They all know cwd. None of them know "what branch were you on?" or "which commit was HEAD?". This is the most-obvious unclaimed flag in the entire shell-history space.

Why nobody else has done it:
- atuin/mcfly are git-agnostic; they'd need to shell out to git per command
- chevrond ALREADY watches git, so adding 2 columns is ~3 LoC
- The query surface unlocks "what did I do on this branch last month" trivially

This is the single highest-ROI item in the frontier list.

## Surface

- Schema bump v2 → v3:
  - commands.branch TEXT (NULL when not in a git repo)
  - commands.head_sha TEXT (NULL when not in a git repo)
  - Index on (branch, started_at) for the common "history on this branch" query
- chevron history filter additions:
  - `--branch NAME` exact-match
  - `--commit SHA` exact-match (head_sha = SHA)
  - `--exclude-branch NAME` (sibling of existing --exclude-* flags)

## Acceptance criteria

- New rows in a git repo have populated branch + head_sha columns
- New rows outside a git repo have NULL for both
- `chevron history --branch master` filters correctly
- Existing rows pre-migration have NULL for both (no retro-fill)
- Schema migration tested (v2→v3 idempotent)
- Snapshot-tested with fixture commits across multiple branches

## Effort

S (~250 LoC + schema + tests). The git2 lookup is already done elsewhere in the codebase (RepoStatus::compute), so this is mostly composition.

## Synergy

- chevrond's state.rs CmdStart handler is the insertion point
- src/segments/git.rs has the branch-discovery logic to reuse
- Existing schema-migration pattern (chevron-1yn.4) is the template

## Future-coupled

The Phase 8 sync spike (chevron-wvn) should preserve branch + head_sha across sync. Note in that spike's design.

## Out of scope

- Storing remote branch names (origin/feature/x). Just local branch.
- Storing the commit message. Just the SHA. Reconstruct full info via `git show <sha>` if needed at query time.
- Recording branch CHANGES (cd into different branch mid-command). Use the branch at CmdStart time; if it changes mid-command, history still shows the start branch.

title: Tier 1: Ready frontier features (branch context, per-cwd memory, cross-pane awareness)
description:
Three features that are cheap to implement (each S-M effort), build entirely on chevron's existing infrastructure, and have NO competitor analogue. Genuinely "nobody has done this" wins.

## Children

- Branch + commit context on every history row (S) — Atuin/mcfly only have cwd. chevron already watches git. Adding `branch` and `head_sha` columns enables `chevron history --branch feature/x`. Embarrassingly easy, ~2 git2 calls per CmdStart.
- Per-cwd memory hint (M) — chpwd hook → chevrond query → one-line recap on every cd into a remembered directory.
- Cross-pane command awareness via tmux pane id (M) — pane 1's failed cargo build chips into pane 2's prompt.

## Why these three together

Each isolated is novel. Together they paint chevron's "shell-aware session brain" story concretely:
- Branch context: chevron knows WHERE in your git history each command was run
- Per-cwd memory: chevron knows WHEN you were last here and what you did
- Cross-pane: chevron knows what's happening in OTHER shells

That trio is unique and individually buildable in 1-3 sessions each.

## Sequence

Branch context first (smallest, enables follow-on filters). Per-cwd memory second. Cross-pane awareness last (uses Phase 3 subscribe channel).

title: Frontier features: things nobody has done in this space
description:
After three rounds of competitor research (Starship/p10k, atuin, mcfly+fzf), the parity gaps are filed (chevron-1yp, chevron-1yq). This epic captures the SECOND-DERIVATIVE question: things in chevron's adjacent possible that no competitor has built.

The frontier here is the intersection of chevron's existing infrastructure:
- Persistent daemon (chevrond) with FS watch
- Captured stdout/stderr bytes (chevron-1yn.4)
- Live event push to shells (chevron-1yn.3)
- Native git awareness (libgit2 directly linked)
- Multi-shell hook coverage (zsh/bash/fish)
- Open-source local-first (no trust constraints that closed competitors face)

Reference: session transcript 2026-05-23 "frontier features" discussion. 9 candidate ideas in 3 tiers.

## Sub-epics (tiers by risk)

- chevron-1ys.1: Tier 1 — Ready features (cheap + immediately tractable + clearly differentiating)
- chevron-1ys.2: Tier 2 — Design spikes (novel, need design before implementation)
- chevron-1ys.3: Tier 3 — Long-shot research (might never ship, but flag the territory)

## Positioning

These aren't responses to competitor pressure. They're "what does chevron's stack make uniquely possible that no one else can do?" The answer: a lot, especially at the intersection of {persistent daemon, captured output, git awareness, live-push channel}.

## Success criteria

A user describes chevron as "the one with [X]" where X is a feature competitors can't credibly add because of architectural choices they've made. The Tier 1 features alone should produce at least one such X.

## Out of scope

- AI agent hooks. chevron isn't an AI product. (Local LLM completion in Tier 3 is different — it's a privacy-preserving alternative to cloud-based tools, not an "AI assistant.")
- Full PTY ownership a la Warp. The cost-benefit doesn't make sense given OSC 133 + capture get most of the value.
- Replacement of any existing competitor wholesale. Frontier features are additive on top of chevron's existing parity-with-the-rest.

title: Click-feedback table for ranking signal (TUI selections)
description:
Introduce a write path for "user selected this command from the TUI" events so future ranking models have a click signal to learn from. mcfly's `selected_commands` table is the canonical reference; atuin currently lacks this and so its ranking has to rely on frequency + recency alone.

## Why this matters

The single most important signal for ranking command history is "did the user actually want this command when offered?" — i.e., a click in the TUI. mcfly's neural-ranking research (chevron-vs-mcfly comparison, 2026-05-23) showed that the FEATURE that matters most is `selected_dir_factor` (was this cmd clicked when in this directory before?) — the nonlinearity adds 5-10% over a hand-tuned linear scorer.

If chevron-xkz (TUI) ships without writing selections from day one, every selection prior to a future "now record selections" PR is lost forever. This issue ensures the TUI is built with the selection-record path in place.

## Surface

- New SQLite table `commands_selections`:
  - id (ULID, distinct from commands.id since one cmd can be selected many times)
  - cmd_id (FK to commands.id)
  - selected_at (unix-epoch-ms)
  - selected_cwd (cwd at the moment of selection — may differ from commands.cwd)
  - source (enum: tui_enter | tui_alt_enter | tui_tab — distinguishes "I picked this and ran" vs "I picked this to edit")
- Schema bump v2 → v3 with ALTER TABLE compatible migration (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS — pure additive)
- New wire opcode `RECORD_SELECTION` on chevrond (similar shape to CMD_END)
- New client-side helper in chevron-xkz that issues RECORD_SELECTION when user picks a row

## Acceptance criteria

- Schema migration v2 → v3 adds the table without rewriting existing rows
- `chevron history --show-output <id>` still works (read-only history path unaffected)
- `chevron search` (chevron-xkz) issues RECORD_SELECTION on Enter, Alt-Enter, and Tab
- Daemon handles the new opcode silently (no ACK back to client — fire-and-forget like CMD_END)
- New columns visible in `chevron history --json` output as `selection_count` aggregate (optional v1 — could defer to chevron-dfd / learned-ranking issues)
- Snapshot-tested with fixture selections

## Effort

S (~250 LoC + schema migration + tests).

## Depends on / blocks

- BLOCKS: chevron-xkz (Interactive search TUI) — TUI must record selections from day one
- BLOCKS: future learned-ranking issue (depends on selection data existing)
- Sibling to: chevron-dfd (Frecency scoring) — frecency can use selection count as one of its inputs once this table exists

## Synergy

Same shape as Phase 1's CMD_START / CMD_END: client-mints ULID, daemon writes async, ACK silent. Schema migration follows the chevron-1yn.4 pattern (additive ALTER TABLE detected via meta.schema_version, bump 2→3).

## Reference

mcfly's `selected_commands` table in github.com/cantino/mcfly. Schema is similar (cmd_id, dir, when). Chevron's variant adds `source` to distinguish hard-execute from edit-then-execute (different signal strength).

## Out of scope

- Click decay / time-windowed selection counts. Just record every selection; aggregation logic lives in ranking issues.
- Negative signals ("user picked a different command after seeing this one"). Possible future signal but not v1.
- Sync of selection records. Phase 8 sync is a separate spike (chevron-wvn).

title: chevron doctor diagnostic subcommand
description:
Promote internal src/health/ to a user-facing 'chevron doctor' subcommand. Dump the support-bundle that users need when filing bugs.

## Surface

'chevron doctor' prints to stdout (human-readable) or with --json (machine-readable):
- chevron CLI version (cargo-pkg-version, build date if available)
- chevron daemon version (VERSION opcode round-trip; flags drift)
- Shell type (\$SHELL / detection)
- OS + kernel version
- Socket dir path + chevrond.sock + chevrond.lock existence
- commands.db path + schema_version + row count + size
- outputs/ dir size + file count + total disk usage
- Config file location + parsed config snapshot (with --no-secrets that masks API keys etc.)
- Recent log file tail (last 50 lines from chevrond's log if logging enabled)
- chevron health checks (reuse src/health/checks.rs)

## Acceptance criteria

- 'chevron doctor' returns 0 when nothing's wrong
- Returns non-zero when a critical issue is detected (e.g. daemon-CLI version drift, schema mismatch, lock-stuck socket)
- --json produces parseable output
- --no-secrets masks API keys / passwords in config dump

## Effort

S (~250 LoC + tests).

## Synergy

Most of the data already exists somewhere:
- VERSION opcode (chevron daemon version path) — already done
- Schema check (history.rs check_schema) — exists
- src/health/checks.rs — exists, just needs user-facing exposure
- daemon paths (src/daemon/paths.rs) — exists

This issue is mostly composition. Implementing it is one new src/doctor.rs and a CLI subcommand.

## Reference

atuin's 'atuin doctor' + 'atuin info' are the model. See https://docs.atuin.sh/cli/reference/doctor/.
design:
# chevron doctor — design

User-facing diagnostic subcommand. Reuses health::{Check, Severity} but adds section grouping. Output: text (default), --json (bug reports), --no-color, --check <name>, --fast.

## Sections + checks

### v1 (target ~400 LoC)

**Environment**
- os: OS name + kernel/version (from sysinfo + uname)
- shell: detected from $SHELL
- term: TERM + COLORTERM (warn if TERM=dumb or empty COLORTERM)
- locale: LANG / LC_ALL (warn if not UTF-8)
- glyph: info-level row that PRINTS the powerline arrow (U+E0B0), a 256-color block, and a Nerd Font glyph for the user to eyeball. "If these don't look right, install a Nerd Font."

**Chevron**
- chevron version: CARGO_PKG_VERSION
- chevron path: argv[0] resolved (the binary running this check)
- libgit2: git2::Version::get() (major.minor.patch)
- daemon feature: built-in or not (cfg!(feature = "daemon"))

**Shell integration**
- zshrc / bashrc / fish config grep for `chevron init` (warn if not found AND $SHELL matches)
- starship.toml grep for `chevron` (info-level if found)
- tmux.conf grep for `chevron tmux-title` (info-level if found)

**Self-test (info-only)**
- Raw `chevron path` output verbatim (so users can paste into bug reports)
- Raw `chevron git` output verbatim

### v2 (future, ~+300 LoC)

- Perf benchmark: 5 runs each of path/git/prompt, report median + p95, warn if prompt > 50ms, critical if > 200ms.
- Async cache: if CHEVRON_CACHE_FILE set, verify writable + PWD-consistent.
- Daemon round-trip: use daemon::client::try_version, distinguish socket-missing / stale / version-drift / OK.
- commands.db: schema_version, row count, file size (from history::check_schema).
- outputs/ dir: size + file count + oldest entry.
- Config dump: parse config, print effective values, --no-secrets to mask API keys.

## File layout

Single file `src/doctor.rs` for v1 (~400 LoC fits in one file, matches health/ structure if it grows). Wired via `Some("doctor") => exit(doctor::run(&args[2..]))` in main.rs.

## Section rendering

Need section grouping that health::render lacks. Two options:
- (a) Add `pub fn render_grouped(sections: &[(&str, &[Check])])` to health::render
- (b) Doctor owns its own renderer, reuses Check struct only.

Go with (b) for v1 — health::render's box-drawing chrome doesn't fit the doctor aesthetic, and a doctor-owned renderer can use plain `[ok]/[warn]/[fail]/[info]` prefixes that scan faster in a bug-report paste.

## Output format (text)

```
chevron doctor

Environment
  [ok]   os         macOS 15.4.1 (arm64)
  [ok]   shell      zsh
  [ok]   term       xterm-256color  COLORTERM=truecolor
  [ok]   locale     en_US.UTF-8
  [info] glyph         <- powerline arrow + 256-color block + nerd-font sample

Chevron
  [ok]   version    0.6.0
  [ok]   path       /Users/mim/.cargo/bin/chevron
  [ok]   libgit2    1.8.4
  [info] daemon     built with daemon feature

Shell integration
  [warn] zshrc      ~/.zshrc does not reference `chevron init`
         hint:      add `eval "$(chevron init zsh)"` to ~/.zshrc
  [info] starship   ~/.config/starship.toml not present
  [info] tmux       ~/.tmux.conf references `chevron tmux-title`

Self-test
  [info] path output:
         <raw path output>
  [info] git output:
         <raw git output>

Summary: 0 critical, 1 warning, 10 ok
```

## Exit codes

0 = clean (all ok or info-only), 1 = warnings present, 2 = critical present. Reuse `health::exit_code` logic.

## Tests

- arg parsing (--json/--no-color/--fast/--check)
- shell-integration grep uses tempfile fixtures (write fake ~/.zshrc, point at it via HOME override)
- glyph row contains the U+E0B0 byte sequence
- self-test section invokes the path renderer and includes its output
- JSON output is parseable + contains all checks + sections

Reuses health::Check+Severity (Info variant added). Heavier checks (perf bench, daemon round-trip, config dump, commands.db, outputs/, --no-secrets) tracked separately in chevron-j1y.

title: Interactive search TUI (chevron search)
description:
Fullscreen interactive search bound to Ctrl-R (or up-arrow, configurable). The single marquee gap with atuin — every chevron evaluator who comes from atuin will look for this first.

## Why this matters

Atuin's reputation is the TUI. Users describe atuin as "a nicer Ctrl-R." Chevron has a richer query surface (chevron history flags) than atuin's CLI but ships only the CLI. Users miss the interactive piece even though they gain everything else.

## Surface

- New 'chevron search' subcommand opens a fullscreen TUI
- Default keybindings (emacs-style):
  - Ctrl-R: cycle filter mode (global / host / directory / workspace)
  - Ctrl-S: cycle search mode (fuzzy / prefix / fulltext)
  - Ctrl-O: open inspector for the highlighted entry
  - Alt-1..9: quick-jump to result by index
  - Ctrl-Y: yank highlighted cmd to clipboard
  - Enter: run the highlighted cmd
  - Tab: edit the highlighted cmd before running
  - Esc / Ctrl-C: exit
- Preview pane: when output is captured for the highlighted entry (output_bytes > 0), preview shows the captured bytes (replay first ~50 lines)
- Inline mode (configurable): renders below the prompt, not fullscreen — useful for users with single-line workflows
- Shell integration: 'chevron init zsh --bind-ctrl-r' emits the keybinding snippet that wires Ctrl-R to chevron search

## Acceptance criteria

- Ctrl-R from a configured shell opens the TUI; result is the cmd populated in the prompt (for Tab-edit) or executed (for Enter)
- Filter-mode cycle visibly changes which rows are listed
- Preview pane shows captured output for rows that have it; empty/grey for rows that don't
- TUI exits cleanly on Esc/Ctrl-C without disturbing terminal state (raw-mode restore via Drop, same pattern as src/capture.rs's TermiosGuard)
- Custom keybinding configurable via [search.keybindings] in config.toml
- Works on zsh; bash + fish integration documented as follow-up

## Effort

L (~1000 LoC + TUI framework integration + tests). Library choice: ratatui (most popular Rust TUI lib, MIT-licensed, mature). Adds ~1MB binary size — acceptable for the user-visible payoff.

## Reference / synergy

- src/history.rs's Filters struct (~line 70-150) is the canonical filter pipeline. The TUI is a presentation layer over the same filters.
- src/capture.rs TermiosGuard pattern for raw-mode setup/restore.
- Preview pane reuses the bytes that --show-output dumps; same code path.

## Out of scope

- Frecency-ranked results (filed separately under chevron-1yq.1.2). TUI v1 uses chronological-descending default ranking, switchable to alphabetical or duration-sorted.
- Multi-select / batch delete. v1 is single-row select.
- Vim keymap. v1 ships emacs default; vim mode lands as follow-up.
notes:
## UX design checklist (from fzf-history-widget research, 2026-05-23)

### Keybindings the TUI MUST inherit from fzf

Muscle-memory bindings every chevron user has used in fzf-history-widget. Missing any = TUI feels broken.

Navigation:
- `Ctrl-J` / `Ctrl-N` / `Down` → next result
- `Ctrl-K` / `Ctrl-P` / `Up` → previous result
- `PgDn` / `PgUp` → page through results

Input-line editing (readline parity):
- `Ctrl-A` → jump to start of query (readline-style, NOT fzf's select-all)
- `Ctrl-E` → jump to end
- `Ctrl-U` → clear query
- `Ctrl-W` → delete previous word
- `Ctrl-H` / `Backspace` → delete previous character
- `Ctrl-D` with non-empty query → delete next char; with empty query → exit

Selection / exit:
- `Enter` → PASTE selected command to LBUFFER, DO NOT auto-execute (fzf-history-widget convention; saves countless `rm -rf` accidents)
- `Esc` / `Ctrl-C` / `Ctrl-G` → abort, restore original line buffer
- `Alt-Enter` (opt-in alternate) → paste + execute immediately

Preview:
- `Ctrl-/` → toggle preview pane visibility
- `Shift-Up` / `Shift-Down` → scroll preview

### Chevron-specific keybindings (innovation slots, semantically aligned with fzf)

- `Ctrl-R` (inside TUI) → cycle SORT (frecency / chronological / hit-count) — matches fzf's `--bind=ctrl-r:toggle-sort` semantic so reflexive double-press feels right
- `Ctrl-S` → cycle FILTER MODE (global / host / directory / workspace)
- `Ctrl-O` → open captured output in full pager
- `Alt-R` → inspector toggle (full metadata: exit, duration, host, cwd, started_at)

Do NOT bind `Tab` to "execute" or "edit" — fzf treats it as multi-select toggle.

### Default mode

- Inline rendering, height 40% (matches fzf default + atuin's `inline_height=40` after their fullscreen-by-default complaints)
- Below `--min-height 20`, fall through to fullscreen automatically
- Configurable via `[search] height = "40%"` (0 = fullscreen)
- Open below cursor, push prompt up only when needed. Restore scrollback cleanly on exit (atuin's early fullscreen polluted scrollback — heavily complained about)

### Preview pane

- Default position: right, 50% width
- Auto-switch to `down,40%` when terminal width < 100 cols (fzf's `auto` pattern)
- Default visibility: ON (chevron's killer feature — actual captured output to preview)
- Content top-to-bottom:
  1. Full cmd (wrapped, not truncated)
  2. Metadata strip: exit (colored — green 0, red non-zero), duration, timestamp, cwd
  3. Captured stdout/stderr tail
- Debounce render by ~50ms on selection change (avoids the lag that fzf users complain about)

### Match highlighting

- Bold + distinct foreground color on matched characters
- Two color slots: `hl` (non-current rows) and `hl+` (current row, brighter)
- Do NOT show numeric match scores (fzf doesn't; users find them noisy)
- Respect `NO_COLOR` env var

### Anti-patterns (do NOT do)

- Auto-execute on Enter
- Pollute scrollback with TUI output
- Block input on preview render (use async + placeholder)
- Show match scores in the result row
- Bind destructive actions to bare keys (delete-from-history needs modifier + confirmation)
- Ignore initial query (TUI must accept `$LBUFFER` as initial filter — drop it and users lose half-typed commands)
- Sort-jitter (when sort changes, keep the highlighted row highlighted across re-sort)
- Hard-code colors (allow `hl` / `hl+` override matching fzf's `--color` syntax)

### Sources

fzf README + key-bindings.zsh:
- https://github.com/junegunn/fzf
- https://github.com/junegunn/fzf/blob/master/shell/key-bindings.zsh

atuin `inline_height` + the fullscreen-default complaints that drove the change:
- https://forum.atuin.sh/t/enable-inline-height-and-compact-ui-by-default/403
- https://forum.atuin.sh/t/tui-opens-below-cursor-thus-shifts-the-prompt-up/405

### Tightly-coupled prerequisite

Click-feedback table (separate issue, depends-on relationship). TUI must write selection events from day one (cmd id + timestamp + cwd at selection) so future ranking can learn from clicks. Adding click capture retroactively means losing historical signal.

title: Search & ranking: interactive TUI, frecency, daemon-side hot fuzzy index
description:
Three features that together close the biggest user-visible gap with atuin: an interactive search UI that replaces Ctrl-R, smarter ranking, and the daemon-side hot index that makes both feel sub-millisecond.

## Children

- Interactive search TUI (chevron search) — L, P2 — the marquee feature
- Frecency scoring for command ranking — M, P3 — best after TUI ships (ranking is most visible in interactive search)
- Daemon-side hot fuzzy index for sub-ms search — M, P4 — best after TUI ships (premature without an interactive caller)

## Work order

1. TUI first. Even with bare ranking, the UX win is overwhelming.
2. Frecency after TUI lands. Ranking algorithm can iterate without re-touching TUI surface.
3. Daemon fuzzy index last. Only pays off when search latency matters (interactive caller).

## Why this matters

Atuin's reputation is the TUI. "Atuin is just a nicer Ctrl-R" is how users describe it. Chevron has a richer query surface than atuin's CLI but ships only the CLI — so a user who switches to chevron loses the interactive piece even though they gain everything else.

## Synergy

All three reuse src/history.rs's existing Filters struct + filter pipeline. The TUI is a presentation layer over the same filters chevron history accepts. Frecency is one new column + one ranking expression. Hot index is a daemon-cached secondary structure indexed on cmd + cwd.

title: atuin data-side parity push: TUI search, recording filters, lifecycle, frecency, diagnostics
description:
chevron-1yn (closed) shipped command lifecycle hooks, the history query CLI, PTY-based output capture, and live event push — putting chevron in atuin-adjacent territory on the data side. But atuin still has features chevron lacks that any user migrating between the two will notice within minutes.

This epic closes the most visible gaps. Reference: full delta analysis in session transcript 2026-05-23 (general-purpose subagent feature comparison vs atuin.sh and docs.atuin.sh).

## Sub-epics

- chevron-1yq.1: Search & ranking — interactive TUI, frecency scoring, daemon-side hot fuzzy index
- chevron-1yq.2: Recording lifecycle — write-time filters, delete/dedup/prune commands
- chevron-1yq.3: Operations & UX polish — chevron doctor, CLI ergonomics, systemd socket activation

## Not in scope for this epic

- E2EE sync to a server. Separate design spike (filed separately). chevron-1yn epic notes already pin the schema-readiness pieces (ULIDs, ms timestamps, hostname+session first-class) so when sync lands the schema doesn't need to migrate.
- Atuin Scripts / Atuin Desktop / Atuin Hub. Out of chevron's "prompt + history" positioning.
- Dotfiles sync (aliases, env, functions). Out of scope.
- AI agent hooks. chevron isn't an AI product.
- Extended shell support (nushell, xonsh, powershell). Decision deferred — file as P4 if you want to expand the shell-coverage matrix.

## Headline findings from the comparison

What chevron has that atuin lacks:
- Captured stdout/stderr search via `chevron history --grep-output` (Phase 4)
- `--show-output <id>` replay
- Per-cwd opt-in PTY-based capture, not just metadata
- Live event push to shells (Phase 3 subscribe)

What atuin has that chevron lacks (this epic):
- Full-screen interactive search TUI with Ctrl-R rebind, preview pane, filter-mode cycle (the marquee gap)
- Frecency-ranked search results
- Write-time `history_filter` / `cwd_filter` regex exclusions
- Delete-by-query / dedup / prune commands
- `atuin doctor` diagnostic dump
- Daemon-side in-memory fuzzy index for sub-ms search

## Success criteria

A user familiar with `Ctrl-R atuin` doesn't bounce off chevron because they can't find an interactive search. A user reviewing their recorded history can delete entries that shouldn't have been recorded. A user filing a bug can paste a `chevron doctor` dump.

title: Configuration wizard (chevron configure)
description:
Interactive TUI: 'chevron configure' walks the user through choices and writes a config.toml. p10k's 'p10k configure' is responsible for a lot of the project's reputation — the gif of someone running it and picking a style is the marketing.

## Why this matters

First-impression UX. A user installing chevron for the first time wants 'chevron configure' to give them a working setup in <2 minutes. Without this, they hit a TOML reference they don't want to read.

## Surface

- 'chevron configure' launches the wizard (uses dialoguer or inquire — both popular TUI libraries)
- Questions, in order:
  1. Prompt style: lean / rainbow / classic / pure / two-line / minimal (preset selector)
  2. One-line or two-line prompt
  3. Icons: Nerd Font / Unicode emoji / ASCII-only
  4. OSC 133 markers: enable / disable
  5. Transient prompt (zsh/fish): enable / disable
  6. Async render (zsh CHEVRON_ASYNC): enable / disable
  7. Command history capture (CHEVRON_HISTORY): enable / disable
  8. Output capture (CHEVRON_CAPTURE_HERE): off / per-cwd / always-on
  9. Live prompt updates (CHEVRON_LIVE): enable / disable
  10. Languages to detect (multi-select)
- Output: writes config.toml to default location with the user's choices

## Acceptance criteria

- Wizard completes in <2 minutes for a new user
- Output config.toml has the choices the user made
- Re-running the wizard offers to update or replace the existing config
- Snapshot test: run with scripted answers, verify config.toml matches expected

## Effort

M (~600 LoC + wizard logic + screenshots/gif for docs).

## Dependencies

Soft dependency on chevron-1yp.3.3 (themes / presets) — the wizard offers preset selection as the first question, so presets need to exist. If presets don't land first, wizard ships with fewer top-level options and just emits a hand-written config.

Soft dependency on chevron-1yp.3.1 (format-string per segment) — wizard can generate format strings users wouldn't write by hand. Without 3.1, wizard just sets enable/disable.

## Library choice

Recommend 'inquire' (~700KB, more modern API) over 'dialoguer' (~1MB, more widely used). Either is acceptable.
design:
# chevron configure — v1 locked design

User-facing wizard. Composes on existing Config + segments::prompt::render. Live preview is the killer feature: every preset/icon choice repaints a one-line preview using the user's actual env.

## Library
inquire (~700KB, modern API, native MultiSelect). Adds one default dep.

## Module layout
src/configure/
  mod.rs            entry, flag parsing, run() -> i32
  answers.rs        AnswerSet data carrier
  presets.rs        Preset enum + from_preset(Preset) -> AnswerSet
  preview.rs        render_preview(&AnswerSet) -> String (one-line ANSI)
  emit.rs           PURE: to_config_toml + to_shell_snippet — snapshot tested
  tui.rs            inquire question fns
  writer.rs         atomic write with .bak backup

## Flags
- `chevron configure`              interactive wizard
- `chevron configure --dry-run`    print what would be written, exit 0
- `chevron configure --preset X`   non-interactive (lean/pure/rainbow/classic/minimal)
- `-h, --help`

## Flow
1. Welcome + glyph preview (font self-check)
2. Pick preset (5 options, all previewed inline above the picker)
3. Icons mode (nerd/unicode/ascii) — preview updates
4. Customize features? Y/n. If Y, prompt for: OSC 133, transient prompt, async render, history capture, output capture, live updates.
5. Languages multi-select (node, python, rust)
6. Review: diff vs existing config.toml, plus shell snippet to copy.
7. Write config.toml (backup to .bak if existed).

## v1 cuts
- No two-line preset (needs linebreak segment — separate follow-up)
- No auto-modify of ~/.zshrc — user pastes snippet (follow-up: --install-shell)
- Icons preference stored but renderer not yet swapped (follow-up: per-segment icon module)

## Testability
emit.rs is pure. Snapshot test: `to_config_toml(AnswerSet::from_preset(Lean))` == golden TOML string. TUI thin wrapper, mostly untested.

## Effort
~750 LoC + tests (target ~250 LoC tests).
close_reason: v1 shipped: chevron configure with 5 presets (lean/pure/rainbow/classic/minimal), live preview using user's real env, inquire-based TUI, --dry-run + --preset for non-interactive use. Pure-functional emit.rs (snapshot-tested) + safe writer with backup. Three v1 cuts (--install-shell, icon swapping, two-line preset) tracked in chevron-rfl.

title: Format-string per segment (config refactor)
description:
Refactor the Segment trait so each segment accepts a user-provided format template. Currently each segment hardcodes its display string (e.g. node.rs emits '{ICON} {version}' directly). Lift that to TOML config so users can rearrange.

## Why this matters

Starship's flagship customisation pattern is per-module format strings: 'format = "[(${symbol})$version]($style)"'. Users invest in their config. Without this, chevron can't accept a Starship-style customisation that users carry between machines.

## Surface

- New 'format' field in [segment.X] config: format = "[\${symbol} \${version}]"
- Template variables vary by segment: \${symbol}, \${version}, \${branch}, \${status}, etc.
- Default format preserved per segment so unconfigured users see no change

## Acceptance criteria

- All existing segments render identically when no 'format' is set (regression-safe — snapshot tests for every segment)
- Custom format string changes the rendered output (snapshot-tested with a fixture user-configured format)
- Template parsing rejects unknown variables with a clear error at config-load time (not at render time)
- Documentation: per-segment template variable reference in README

## Effort

M (~500 LoC including refactoring all segments + tests).

## Why this is a prerequisite

This issue UNBLOCKS chevron-1yp.3.2 (declarative detect_* in TOML) and chevron-1yp.3.4 (the configuration wizard generates format strings). Work order: do this first; then 3.2 and 3.4 can land in parallel.

## Reference

src/segments/registry.rs holds the Segment trait. Each src/segments/*.rs implements it. The refactor touches the trait + every impl + the config loader.

## Out of scope

- Starship-compatible template syntax (we use our own simpler subset). If a user wants full Starship compatibility, they should use Starship.

title: Instant prompt for zsh
description:
p10k's flagship trick: render the previous prompt from cache BEFORE .zshrc finishes loading. Result: shell startup feels instant even with a heavy rc file.

## Mechanism

1. On precmd, write the rendered prompt to a cache file (we already do this for the async fast path — _chevron_cache_file).
2. Early in .zshrc (before any user customisation), cat the cache file as the prompt, redirect stdout/stderr to a buffer, defer to background.
3. When the real .zshrc finishes and the first real prompt renders, replace the cached one with the live prompt.

The intricate part is output buffering during .zshrc init: any stdout/stderr that .zshrc generates needs to be captured and replayed AFTER the cached prompt is replaced — otherwise it clobbers the displayed prompt.

## Surface

- Opt-in via CHEVRON_INSTANT_PROMPT=1
- New 'chevron init zsh --instant-prompt' flag that emits the early-rc snippet
- User installs the early-rc snippet at the TOP of .zshrc (above everything)

## Acceptance criteria

- New zsh session shows the previous prompt within ~50ms of shell start (vs ~500ms-1s for a heavy .zshrc with chevron-1yn enabled)
- Output from .zshrc that prints to stdout/stderr is buffered and replayed correctly after the real prompt takes over
- Disabled by default (CHEVRON_INSTANT_PROMPT=0) leaves shell startup unchanged
- Documentation explains the placement requirement (must be at TOP of .zshrc)

## Effort

L (~600 LoC + tests + integration testing against various .zshrc setups).

## Reference

p10k's source is MIT-licensed and reference-quality. See https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k/blob/master/internal/p10k.zsh — the instant_prompt function and surrounding buffer-replay logic.

## Out of scope

- bash equivalent (bash init shape doesn't support this trick cleanly)
- fish equivalent (fish_greeting exists but isn't the same mechanism)

Document as zsh-only.
close_reason: v1 shipped: chevron init zsh --instant-prompt prints a paste-in top-of-rc snippet that paints the cached prompt before .zshrc finishes loading. Pure zsh ($(<file), no fork). Stdout/stderr captured to /tmp buffer during init, replayed in takeover block at top of _chevron_precmd. zshexit hook restores fds if .zshrc crashes. OSC 133 D emitted to close the spurious prompt region. Multi-shell guard (looks for %{ markers). Doctor adds presence check. 11 new tests. zsh -n parse-clean. No env var gate (presence = opt-in). v1 limitations documented: terminal wrap, interactive reads, % in paths. ~180 LoC. Configure-v2 (chevron-rfl) will add wizard integration.

title: Right prompt (RPROMPT) support across shells
description:
Add right-side prompt rendering. zsh has RPROMPT natively, fish has fish_right_prompt; bash needs a cursor-position trick (set RPROMPT-like content via cursor positioning in PROMPT).

## Surface

- New [right_prompt] section in config.toml mirroring the existing prompt config (segment list, ordering, format strings)
- New 'chevron rprompt' subcommand renders the right side
- Shell init scripts wire it up:
  - zsh: 'RPROMPT="\$(chevron rprompt …)"'
  - fish: 'function fish_right_prompt; chevron rprompt …; end'
  - bash: cursor-position trick (documented limitation: doesn't survive command-output overflow as cleanly as zsh/fish)

## Acceptance criteria

- zsh: RPROMPT renders on the right edge, updates on precmd
- fish: fish_right_prompt renders on the right edge
- bash: rendered with cursor-positioning (documented limitation)
- Default config has [right_prompt] empty (off by default)
- Integration test: `chevron rprompt` subcommand exits 0 and emits a renderable string

## Effort

M (~400 LoC including shell wiring + tests).

## Reference

The existing src/segments/prompt.rs is the closest template. The segment registry needs to support an "ordered list per side" rather than a single list. See src/segments/registry.rs (or wherever the ordered list lives).

## Synergy

Daemon-cached RepoStatus is reused for the right side at zero added cost. A second render-pass over the same cached data.

## Out of scope

- Right-prompt-specific segments (we ship the same set, configurable per-side).
- RPS1 (right primary string in zsh, a separate slot). Standard RPROMPT is what nearly everyone uses.

title: Go runtime segment
description:
Detect Go via ancestor lookup for go.mod or *.go in cwd. Display the version from .go-version, GOROOT (parse dir name), or the toolchain line in go.mod ('go 1.22').

## Surface

- Segment name: golang
- Default icon: Nerd Font gopher glyph (configurable via config.toml icon override)
- Default format: '{ICON} {version}' (matches existing patterns)
- Detection precedence: .go-version → go.mod 'go X.Y' line → GOROOT env

## Acceptance criteria

- Renders nothing when no Go ancestor file is found within cwd → home
- Renders 'go 1.22' style version when go.mod present
- Renders the .go-version content if present
- Falls back gracefully (empty segment) if all sources are missing
- Snapshot-tested via insta against fixture project trees

## Reference

src/segments/rust_toolchain.rs is the closest template — also reads a per-language version file. Effort: S (~150 LoC + tests).

## Out of scope

- Invoking 'go env GOVERSION' (slow on cold start, ~100ms). v1 reads only files and env vars. If a user has neither, no segment — acceptable.
- Module-path display. Just version.

## Synergy
