Daemon caching of parsed go.mod by (path + mtime) is a 4.x follow-up if invoking 'go env' becomes the only viable source. Not needed for v1.

title: .tool-versions / asdf-mise universal segment
description:
A SINGLE segment that reads .tool-versions (asdf format) and .mise.toml (mise format) from cwd ancestors and renders versions for every tool listed. Multiplier covering Go, Ruby, Java, Node, Python, Erlang, Elixir, Lua, Perl, PHP, Scala, Crystal, Deno, Rust, Terraform, Helm, kubectl, etc. without writing one segment per language.

## Why prioritise over per-language segments

A user with .tool-versions gets 10+ languages' versions from one segment. Implementing each language individually would be 7+ S-sized issues; this is one M-sized issue. Filing under chevron-1yp.5 (under "VCS / multi-tool") rather than .1 (per-language runtimes) because it's structurally a single segment, not language-specific.

## Surface

- Segment name: tool_versions
- Display modes (configurable):
  - 'compact': comma-separated 'tool/version' pairs (e.g. 'nodejs/20.10 ruby/3.2.1 go/1.21.0')
  - 'multi': one sub-segment per tool listed (lets users pick which to show via tools allowlist)
- Config: 'tools' allowlist so users can hide entries they don't want (default: show all)

## Acceptance criteria

- Reads .tool-versions in cwd or any ancestor up to home
- Reads .mise.toml similarly (toml::Value already in dependencies)
- Combines both files if both exist; newer file wins for overlapping tools
- Snapshot-tested with fixture files containing multiple tools
- Renders nothing if neither file exists in cwd or ancestors

## Out of scope

- Validating that the listed versions are actually installed. Just read the version specifier from the file. Validation is asdf/mise's job.

## Effort

M (~300 LoC + tests).

## Synergy

chevrond can cache parsed .tool-versions keyed by (file path + mtime). File as a Phase 5.x follow-up only if performance measurement shows it matters; v1 reads on each render (.tool-versions files are tiny, <1KB).

## Filed under chevron-1yp.5 (VCS / multi-tool sub-epic)

Reasoning: structurally not a language segment but a multi-tool reader. Logical sibling to a hypothetical hg/jj segment.

title: Ruby runtime segment
description:
Detect Ruby via ancestor lookup for Gemfile, Gemfile.lock, Rakefile, or *.rb. Display the version from .ruby-version, .tool-versions (ruby line), RBENV_VERSION, CHRUBY_VERSION, or rbenv version output.

## Surface

- Segment name: ruby
- Default icon: Nerd Font ruby gem glyph
- Default format: '{ICON} {version}'
- Detection precedence: .ruby-version → .tool-versions ruby line → RBENV_VERSION → CHRUBY_VERSION → skip

## Acceptance criteria

- Renders nothing without a Ruby ancestor file or active rbenv/chruby env
- Renders '3.2.1' style version when .ruby-version present
- Reads .tool-versions ruby line as fallback (single file, multiple version managers)
- Snapshot-tested via insta

## Reference

src/segments/node.rs is the closest template — similar version-file ancestor pattern with multiple precedence layers.

## Effort

S (~150 LoC + tests).

## Synergy

chevron-1yp.5.1 (.tool-versions universal segment) covers the .tool-versions case alongside Go/Ruby/Java/etc. all at once. If 5.1 lands first, this segment's .tool-versions fallback layer is redundant — but a Ruby-only user without .tool-versions still benefits from this dedicated segment.

title: Shell experience parity (RPROMPT, instant prompt, OS icon)
description:
Three industry-standard shell-experience features that chevron lacks. Each is a different category of gap:

- Right-prompt (RPROMPT): the single biggest user-visible gap. Many users put time/k8s/aws on the right.
- Instant prompt: p10k's flagship trick — render a cached prompt BEFORE .zshrc finishes loading. Most users with heavy rc files notice 500ms-1s+ of shell startup; instant prompt cuts that to ~50ms perceived.
- OS icon: polish. Trivial to add, popular look.

## Children

- chevron-1yp.2.1: Right prompt (RPROMPT) support across shells
- chevron-1yp.2.2: Instant prompt for zsh (zsh-only by nature)
- chevron-1yp.2.3: OS icon segment

## Priority order

1. Right prompt first — biggest visible UX gap.
2. Instant prompt second — biggest performance perception gap, but L-sized effort.
3. OS icon last — polish, S-sized.

## Out of scope

- Vi-mode indicator. chevron is multi-shell and the value asymmetry is too zsh-tilted to justify per-shell complexity. Filing as separate issue only if a user asks.
- Continuation prompt (PS2). Low impact for most users; revisit if requested.

title: Configurability v2: format-string, declarative detection, themes, wizard, conditional visibility
description:
Five features that lift chevron's config from "hard-coded per segment" to "user-customisable like Starship and p10k": format strings per segment, declarative file detection in TOML, theme presets, an interactive wizard, and conditional segment visibility based on the about-to-run command.

## Children (with dependencies)

- chevron-1yp.3.1: Format-string per segment (M; PREREQUISITE for 3.2 and helpful for 3.4)
- chevron-1yp.3.2: detect_files / detect_folders / detect_extensions in TOML (S; depends on 3.1)
- chevron-1yp.3.3: Themes / presets bundled with `chevron init --preset rainbow|minimal|pure`
- chevron-1yp.3.4: Configuration wizard (`chevron configure`)
- chevron-1yp.3.5: show_on_command conditional segment visibility

## Work order

1. 3.1 (format-string refactor) first — unblocks 3.2 and reduces friction for 3.4.
2. 3.3 (presets) and 3.4 (wizard) in parallel — independent, both user-facing.
3. 3.2 (declarative detect_*) once 3.1 lands.
4. 3.5 (show_on_command) last — uses Phase 1 cmd-lifecycle stash, independent of the rest.

## Why this matters

A user who has invested time in a Starship or p10k config can't carry that to chevron without rewriting most of it. Closing this gap makes chevron migration tractable. The wizard alone (chevron-1yp.3.4) is responsible for a lot of p10k's reputation — "watch this gif, choose a style, done."

title: Language runtime segment expansion (Go, Ruby, Java, .NET, .tool-versions)
description:
chevron currently has segments for python, nodejs, and rust. Starship has ~30 language modules; p10k has a similar count. The "where's Go?" objection is the most common one a new user comparing the three will raise.

## Why this matters

A polyglot user evaluating chevron will land on a project repo and expect to see their language's version in the prompt without configuration. Today they don't. Closing this is table-stakes parity.

## Implementation pattern

Each language segment is ~100 LoC against the existing src/segments/node.rs / python.rs / rust_toolchain.rs templates. Standard shape:

1. Ancestor-file detection (e.g. go.mod, Gemfile, pom.xml) — there's a helper for this already
2. Version read from a project version file (.go-version, .ruby-version, etc.)
3. Optional env-var fallback (RBENV_VERSION, GOROOT, etc.)
4. Display with a Nerd Font icon + version string

## Children (each independently shippable)

- chevron-1yp.1.1: Go segment (go.mod, .go-version)
- chevron-1yp.1.2: Ruby segment (Gemfile, .ruby-version, rbenv/chruby)
- chevron-1yp.1.3: Java segment (pom.xml, build.gradle, .java-version)
- chevron-1yp.1.4: .NET segment (*.csproj, global.json)
- chevron-1yp.5.1 (under sub-epic .5): .tool-versions / asdf-mise universal — single segment covering 10+ languages

## Out of scope

Niche languages (Mojo, Solidity, Typst, Crystal, Nim, Zig, V, Pijul, Fennel). Land via user-driven requests, not preemptive coverage. Same call Starship would make if starting today.

## Synergy

The .tool-versions / mise universal segment (chevron-1yp.5.1, filed under the languages sub-epic .5 but really an asdf-multiplier) covers many languages at once. Worth doing it FIRST if work order matters — it's the multiplier.

title: Feature parity push: close the obvious gaps vs Starship and Powerlevel10k
description:
The chevron-1yn epic (closed) put chevron in different territory than Starship/p10k on the DATA side: command-lifecycle history (Phase 1), atuin-shape query CLI (Phase 2), live-prompt push (Phase 3), PTY-based output capture (Phase 4). The competitive angle there is "shell-aware session brain" — close to Atuin/Warp, not Starship.

On the PROMPT-RENDERING side, chevron is still well behind on basic feature coverage that any user comparing the three projects will notice within 30 seconds.

## Goal

Close the most visible feature gaps so a user evaluating chevron against Starship or p10k doesn't bounce off because of missing-language-segment or missing-RPROMPT objections. Keep the data-side work as the distinctive angle; this epic just brings the prompt rendering to peer level.

## Themed sub-epics

- chevron-1yp.1: Language runtime segment expansion (Go, Ruby, Java, .NET + .tool-versions multiplier)
- chevron-1yp.2: Shell experience parity (RPROMPT, instant prompt, OS icon)
- chevron-1yp.3: Configurability v2 (format-string per segment, declarative detect_*, themes/presets, configuration wizard, show_on_command)
- chevron-1yp.4: System resource segments (battery, memory/load/disk via existing health/ subsystem)
- chevron-1yp.5: VCS expansion (Jujutsu jj — hg/fossil/pijul deferred as too niche)

## Reference

Full delta analysis in session transcript 2026-05-23 (general-purpose subagent feature comparison against Starship docs at starship.rs/config/ and Powerlevel10k README). Headline findings:
- Language segments: chevron has 3 (python/node/rust); Starship and p10k each have ~30.
- RPROMPT, instant prompt, configuration wizard: all three are industry-standard, chevron lacks all three.
- Format-string per segment + declarative detect_* are standard customisation patterns chevron doesn't expose.

## Positioning

Not in scope: rebranding chevron as a "starship competitor." chevron's competitive angle is the daemon + history + capture stack. This epic is hygiene — close visible gaps so the prompt-rendering side feels modern. Past the gap-closing, no need to chase per-segment parity with Starship's ~80 modules.

## Success criteria

A user who opens https://starship.rs and the chevron README side-by-side doesn't spot an obvious "this is missing" within 60 seconds of scanning. Specifically:
- Polyglot users with asdf/mise get version readouts for ~10 languages without configuration.
- Users with k8s/aws/time on the right of their prompt can configure that.
- Users with heavy .zshrc don't feel chevron slowing down shell startup.
- New users have a < 2 minute path from install to a working prompt via the wizard.

title: Phase 3.4: chevron subscribe auto-reconnect
description: Currently when the daemon restarts (chevron daemon stop / start or crash), the subscriber's read end gets EOF and the helper exits cleanly. The shell's zle -F callback notices via the unregister-on-EOF path and the user loses live updates until they open a new shell. Add a reconnect loop with backoff: on EOF, sleep + retry the connection up to N times. Bounded so a permanently-dead daemon doesn't pin the helper.
close_reason: Implemented helper-side reconnect with bounded exponential backoff (100ms..2s, 10-attempt budget that resets on each successful subscription) in src/subscribe.rs: connect->handshake->relay wrapped in a reconnect loop so the shell fd stays open across daemon restarts. Initial no-daemon contract (exit 2) preserved. Reconnect policy + backoff extracted to pure next_action/backoff_delay and unit-tested deterministically (6 new tests); clippy clean; ffu.1 e2e still green. Daemon-restart e2e tracked as chevron-ffu.5.

title: Phase 4: Opt-in output capture
description:
preexec wraps the command in a script(1)-style capture, ships stdout/stderr bytes to chevrond. Per-cwd opt-in via config (`CHEVRON_CAPTURE_OUTPUT_HERE=1` env or config-file glob).

## Schema additions
Output stored separately (`outputs` table or files keyed by command id). Sized-bounded: drop oldest beyond N MB per session, or use rotating files.

## Query
`chevron history --grep PATTERN` searches captured output. Powerful for 'find the build where I saw that error'.

## Tradeoffs
- Costs: per-command perf overhead (tee through script), disk for outputs.
- Mitigations: opt-in, per-directory enable, size cap, age cap.

## Blocked by
Phase 1 (need command ids to attach output to).
close_reason: Shipped in feat(capture): PTY-interposing output capture (commit 3bb5c85 on unstable); follow-ups filed as chevron-1yn.4.x children

title: Phase 3: Live prompt segments — subscribe to chevrond events
description:
Prompt segments that update mid-prompt-life via chevrond notifications. Reuses the async cache infrastructure already shipped (chevron-7fs.5).

## Mechanism
- chevrond publishes events (file change in cwd, branch update from background fetch, command N+1 finished elsewhere) to interested subscribers.
- Shell init opens a subscriber connection per session.
- On event, the existing zle -F + cache-file path triggers a redraw.

## Use cases
- Background `git fetch` finishes: branch indicator updates with new ahead/behind.
- File watcher fires: modified-file count refreshes without a new command.
- Sibling pane finished a build: optional notification chip in the prompt.

## Smart completion / arg-frequency (gosh-inspired)
Once we have the command log (phase 1) we can build the gosh-style arg-frequency table on top. Inline ghost suggestion in the prompt: "after `cargo test` you usually `git status` — Tab". Big phase-3+ feature; file separately when we get there.

## Blocked by
Phase 1.
close_reason: Shipped in feat(daemon): live prompt subscriber + push-direction wire (commit aba79c5 on unstable); follow-ups filed as chevron-1yn.3.x children

title: Phase 2: chevron history — structured query CLI
description:
Once phase 1 is shipping events, expose a query interface.

## Command surface
`chevron history [--cwd PATH] [--failed] [--since DUR] [--limit N] [--format FMT]`

- `--cwd`: filter by working dir (default: anywhere).
- `--failed`: only nonzero exit.
- `--since`: relative time ("1h", "yesterday", "monday").
- `--limit`: cap rows.
- `--format`: table (default), json, jsonl, ndjson, tsv.

## Output
Default table: `time | cwd | exit | dur | cmd`. JSON option for piping.

## Backed by
SQLite query against the table from phase 1. chevrond exposes a HISTORY opcode over the existing UDS so the client doesn't need direct DB access (avoids file lock contention with the daemon).

## Reference
gosh has shape inspiration for query patterns
(github.com/mmichie/gosh — arg-frequency-by-prefix); ours operates
on the linear command log, not arg frequencies (that's phase 3+).

## Blocked by
Phase 1 (need the events / table).
close_reason: Shipped in feat(history): query CLI (commit efae3bd on unstable); follow-ups filed as 2.1-2.6 children

title: Phase 1: Shell-side command lifecycle hooks publish to chevrond
description:
Shell init hooks publish command-lifecycle events to chevrond over the existing UDS. This is the foundational data flow; everything downstream consumes from chevrond.

## Events
- `command-started`: cwd, cmd line, start timestamp, session id, hostname.
- `command-finished`: same id, exit status, end timestamp, duration_ms.

## Schema (chevrond side)
SQLite, in chevrond's existing socket dir. Columns mirror events:
`id, session_id, hostname, cwd, cmd, started_at, finished_at,
duration_ms, exit_status`. Indexes on cwd, started_at, exit_status.

## Wire protocol
Extends the existing line-oriented chevrond protocol. New opcodes:
- `CMD_START <session> <cwd> <cmd...>` -> `OK <id>`
- `CMD_END <id> <exit> <duration_ms>` -> `OK`

Encoding: percent-escape the cmd + cwd same as STATUS path arg.

## Shell hooks (zsh first)
- preexec: emit CMD_START. Stash returned id.
- precmd: emit CMD_END with exit + computed duration.

Bash + fish parallels in the existing init files.

## Out of scope here
- No output capture (phase 4).
- No query CLI (phase 2).
- No live segment subscription (phase 3).
- chevrond's SQLite ddl + connection pooling are the bulk of the work.
notes:
## Sync-readiness pins (added after design review)

The eventual roadmap includes Atuin-style sync to a remote server (Phase 8+). Phase 1 schema choices that keep that door open at zero current cost:

- **Use string IDs (ULID or UUIDv7), not INTEGER autoincrement.** Globally unique, sortable-by-time, won't collide across machines.
- **Store timestamps as INTEGER unix-epoch milliseconds**, not SQLite's CURRENT_TIMESTAMP (text-formatted, locale-prone). Atuin uses nanos; ms is fine.
- **Keep hostname + session_id as first-class columns** (already in the design).
- **Output (Phase 4) goes in a separate `outputs` table** keyed by command id, not as a column on `commands`. Lets output stay machine-local even if commands sync later.
- **Add a `meta` table** with at minimum a `schema_version` row. Migrations later become surgical.
- **Respect secret-hiding patterns from day one**: commands starting with space (`HISTCONTROL=ignorespace`) should not be captured. `HISTIGNORE` patterns too if cheap. This avoids accidentally banking secrets that then get synced.
close_reason: Shipped in feat(daemon): command lifecycle hooks (commit 4be81a7 on unstable)

title: Structured session knowledge — chevrond as session brain
description:
## Vision

Evolve chevron from a prompt-decoration tool into a structured-session-knowledge system. chevrond (already exists for git status caching) becomes the central record of what the user has done: commands run, exit statuses, durations, cwd at the time, output (optionally captured). Shells emit lifecycle events; chevrond stores them; a query interface (`chevron history …`) and live prompt segments consume the data.

This is the natural extension of the OSC 133 + async + transient work already shipped — the infrastructure is mostly there, we just need to push state into the daemon and surface it.

## Why now

The OSC 133 + cursor-rewrite struggle (chevron-4xq) was a tell. We kept hitting the wall of "shell-as-byte-stream has no memory." Powerlevel10k's maintainer admits the working impl is 'very complex' and still has the off-by-one. A daemon that remembers state cuts past those tricks entirely.

## Phases (each shippable standalone)

1. **Command lifecycle capture** (chevron-7fs.5 follow-on shape):
   shell hooks publish `command-started`/`command-finished` events
   to chevrond over the existing UDS. Schema captures cwd, cmd, exit,
   duration, timestamp, hostname, session id. SQLite-backed.
2. **`chevron history` query CLI**: structured queries against the
   command log. `chevron history --failed --since=1d`, scoped to cwd
   or session, replays past commands, etc.
3. **Live prompt segments**: subscribe to chevrond events. Branch
   indicator updates on background fetch. Modified-file count updates
   on filesystem changes. Reuses the existing async cache mechanism.
4. **Output capture (opt-in)**: preexec wraps the command in
   `script(1)`-style capture, ships bytes to chevrond. Per-cwd
   opt-in. Unlocks output search.

## Long-horizon (deliberately not committing now)

5. Terminal cooperation via extended OSC for region updates (works
   with Wezterm/Ghostty maintainers).
6. Output folding / rich UX.
7. PTY ownership (Warp-style) — probably never. The value is the
   daemon's knowledge, not the rendering.

## Wild ideas worth noting (do NOT file as work)

- Time-travel prompt: scroll past a command, hit a key, the prompt
  redraws as it WAS at that moment (using historical git state +
  cwd).
- Counterfactual prompt: "show me this prompt as if I were on main"
  without checking out.
- Diff prompts: between two prompts in scrollback, show what changed
  (branch moved, files added, env changed). Mini commit graph for
  the session.
- Predictive segment: chevrond learns patterns ("after `cargo
  test` you usually `git status`") and inline-suggests.
- Session DAG: organise commands as graph by cwd + branch + parent
  command rather than linear time.

These are emergent properties of phases 1–4 being built well; revisit
once the data exists.

## Reference

User has prior art in their `gosh` shell (github.com/mmichie/gosh).
Notable: gosh uses SQLite for per-command argument-frequency tracking
to power smart completion (`commands` + `arguments` tables with
`count` + `last_used`). That's phase 3+ flavour, not phase 1, but
shape and indexes are worth referencing when we get there. gosh does
NOT do full-command history with exit codes / duration / output —
that's net-new work for chevron.

## Positioning

This shifts chevron's identity somewhat: from 'powerline prompt
segments' to 'shell-aware session brain'. Competition becomes Atuin
(structured history), Warp (closed-source full takeover), Fig.
Differentiator: open source, daemon-backed, terminal-agnostic, tight
git/repo integration via existing chevrond gitwatcher.
notes:
## Sync / remote (long horizon, NOT in phases 1-4)

Worth noting now: the eventual long-term goal includes Atuin-style E2EE sync. Two paths when we get there:

- **Atuin-compatible**: implement atuin's wire + encryption protocol. Users can push to an existing atuin server (their own or atuin.sh) AND have chevron's local-first daemon. Leverages ecosystem.
- **Chevron-native**: own sync protocol. More flexibility (can model branch state, repo identity, command DAG — things atuin doesn't). More server work.

This is post phases 1-4. Schema pins for it (see Phase 1 notes): string IDs, unix-ms timestamps, separate outputs table, schema_version meta row, secret-hiding from capture-time.
close_reason: All four phases shipped to unstable: 1yn.1 (lifecycle hooks), 1yn.2 (history CLI), 1yn.3 (live prompt subscriber), 1yn.4 (PTY-based output capture). Follow-up work tracked under chevron-1yn.X.x children for each phase.

title: Transient chevron color reflects wrong command's exit (off-by-one)
description:
The transient prompt collapsed by accept-line shows a green/red chevron based on the previous command's exit, not the command on that line (the about-to-run command's exit isn't known yet at collapse time). Visible as 'false' showing green and 'true' showing red in scrollback after a sequence of commands.

## Fix: cursor-rewrite in precmd
- accept-line draws a neutral transient and saves cursor with DECSC (\e7).
- precmd, knowing the exit code, uses \e[s (SCOSC) to stash the current next-prompt position, \e8 (DECRC) to jump back to the transient line, erases and rewrites with the correct color + command text, then \e[u (SCORC) restores the next-prompt position so zsh draws the next PROMPT where it belongs.
- Same fix in fish via fish_preexec (save) + fish_postexec (rewrite).
- Bash already skips transient entirely (no change).

## Known limitations
- Commands that themselves use DECSC/DECRC (vim, less, man, alt-screen apps) clobber the cursor save; the rewrite falls through to the neutral chevron — visible but not actively misleading.
- Commands whose output scrolls the screen past the transient line: the saved row is no longer valid; rewrite lands on the wrong line. Same edge case powerlevel10k accepts.

Reported by Pat after seeing 'false' show green and 'true' show red in his prompt.
close_reason: fixed via cursor-rewrite in precmd. accept-line draws a neutral chevron + saves cursor with DECSC; precmd does SCOSC + DECRC + erase + colored chevron + cmd + SCORC to rewrite the transient line in place with the now-known exit status. Same pattern in fish via fish_postexec. bash already skipped transient. Fragility documented in code: vim/less/etc. that manipulate DECSC clobber our save (rewrite falls through to neutral, not lying); commands whose output scrolled past the transient line glitch. Same edge case set powerlevel10k accepts. 388 lib + 45 integration tests green.

title: Async stale-then-fresh prompt for zsh
description:
Render a stale/cached prompt instantly, background-compute fresh data, redraw with bright styling when ready. The headline latency feature of the chevron-7fs epic.

## Motivation
Today's sync model still pays ~10ms process-spawn per prompt even with the daemon warm. Async hooks let the prompt appear in <5ms (cached fast path) and update when the real compute lands.

## Design
- Add chevron CLI surface for fast cache-only render: 'chevron prompt --stale-ok' returns whatever the daemon has cached without spawning compute or doing inline fallback.
- Or: chevrond writes the rendered prompt to $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/chevron/last-prompt after every successful render; init script reads it via 'cat' (no fork) before kicking off the async update.
- zsh hook fires fast path in precmd, background-spawns fresh compute, signals zle reset-prompt when done (zle -F file descriptor callback).
- Stale data styled with dim colours; bright on update.

## Open questions
- Cache key: cwd alone, or cwd + last_exit + last_duration? Stale-but-wrong-cwd is worse than blocking.
- How to integrate with chevron-7fs.1 transient — order of operations on accept-line.

## Acceptance
- Prompt appears in <5ms regardless of repo size.
- Updates within 100ms of fresh data being ready.
- Falls back gracefully when no cache (cold start).
close_reason: async stale-then-fresh prompt for zsh landed. CHEVRON_CACHE_FILE env var on chevron-side writes the rendered prompt atomically; shell-side _chevron_start_async uses process substitution + zle -F for the background pipe and zle reset-prompt for the redraw. Opt-in via CHEVRON_ASYNC=1 (off by default — most users won't notice the difference vs the existing ~10ms sync, but huge-repo users get a sub-ms perceived latency). 359 lib + 45 integration tests green. Unblocks bash and fish ports.

title: Post-execution prompt rewrite for zsh
description:
After a command completes, rewrite the just-issued (collapsed) prompt line with duration and exit code so scrollback retains timing info for slow commands.

## Motivation
The transient prompt (chevron-7fs.1) leaves a single chevron in scrollback. For slow commands, the user often wants to know how long it took — currently you'd need to scroll back to the running prompt, which is gone. Post-exec rewrite appends '5.2s' (or similar) to the transient line.

## Design
- Save the cursor row when the transient prompt is rendered (in _chevron_accept_line).
- In _chevron_precmd, if duration > threshold (e.g., 2000ms), emit ANSI cursor codes to jump back to the saved row, repaint the transient with duration appended, restore cursor.
- Skip the rewrite when duration is short (avoid scrollback churn for fast commands).
- Threshold configurable via CHEVRON_TRANSIENT_DURATION_MS (default 2000).

## Acceptance
- After 'sleep 3', scrollback shows the transient line with a duration tag.
- After 'ls' (fast), scrollback shows the plain transient chevron.
- Disable transient (CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0) also disables post-exec rewrite.
close_reason: zsh post-exec duration tag landed. Dim duration line in precmd, threshold CHEVRON_TRANSIENT_DURATION_MS (default 2000ms), minute/decimal format pivot at 60s, opt-out via CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0. Design pivot from issue spec (in-place cursor rewrite) to separate-line emission — same user value, robust across terminals. 353 lib + 42 integration tests green.

title: Bash port: transient + post-exec
description:
Port chevron-7fs.1 (transient) and chevron-7fs.2 (post-exec) to bash. Async (7fs.3) is harder in bash and may be deferred or implemented differently.

## Constraints
- Bash has no ZLE; can't override accept-line cleanly.
- Bind to Enter (RETURN) via 'bind -x' or use PROMPT_COMMAND + cursor codes.
- Some terminals may handle the cursor manipulation differently.

## Acceptance
- Transient collapse on Enter, scrollback shows minimal chevron.
- Post-exec duration tag on slow commands.
- CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0 disables both.
close_reason: bash port landed. Post-exec duration tag ported via PROMPT_COMMAND (same format and env-var contract as zsh/fish). Transient prompt deliberately omitted — bash's bind -x and DEBUG-trap workarounds both break on multi-line input, wrapped lines, and compound commands. Same reason powerlevel10k stays zsh-only and Starship doesn't ship a bash transient. A regression test locks in the omission decision. Verified in real bash: duration line appears for slow commands and CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0 export disables it cleanly. 368 lib + 45 integration tests green. This closes the chevron-7fs epic.

title: Fish port: transient + post-exec
description:
Port chevron-7fs.1 (transient) and chevron-7fs.2 (post-exec) to fish.

## Notes
- Fish (since 3.2) has built-in support for transient prompts via the fish_prompt function pattern. Use that instead of cursor gymnastics.
- Fish supports per-event handlers (fish_postexec etc.) which simplify the post-exec rewrite.

## Acceptance
- Transient collapse works using fish's idiomatic mechanism.
- Post-exec duration tag.
- CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0 disables.
close_reason: fish port landed. Transient prompt via Enter binding + flag + commandline -f repaint/execute (the fish-idiomatic pattern, same shape Starship uses). Post-exec duration tag via fish_postexec event + native $CMD_DURATION (no preexec timestamp tracking needed, unlike zsh). Opt-out via CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0. Async stale-then-fresh deliberately excluded — fish needs a different pipe-watching approach and the issue scoped it out. 364 lib + 45 integration tests green.

title: Transient prompt for zsh
description:
On Enter, rewrite the previous prompt line to a minimal form (single chevron or character) so scrollback stays clean and copy-paste captures just the command, not the prompt chrome.

## Motivation
Multi-segment powerline prompts are great for the active line but pollute scrollback. Selecting a command for re-execution forces the user to also grab the prompt. Transient collapse fixes both.

## Design
- ZLE widget wrapping accept-line.
- On accept, use terminal cursor codes (or zsh's zle -I + redraw) to overwrite the just-accepted prompt line with a minimal stub.
- Stub format: simple character like '$' or a single chevron, preserves color hint for success/failure.
- Original full prompt re-renders for the next command.

## Acceptance
- Pressing Enter on a multi-segment prompt leaves only a stub in scrollback.
- The just-issued command is still readable on the same line.
- copy-paste of the previous line yields just the command, no prompt chrome.
- Toggleable via env var (CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0 to disable).
close_reason: zsh transient prompt landed. accept-line ZLE widget swaps PROMPT to colour-coded chevron and reset-prompts before dispatching to the original (or any prior override). Verified in real zsh: widget registration, exit-status-driven colour switching, CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0 opt-out. 350 lib + 42 integration tests green.

title: First publish: crates.io 'chevron' + tag release
description:
Once local rename merged and GitHub repo moved:

1. cargo publish --dry-run --allow-dirty to sanity-check the package contents.
2. cargo login (one-time, with crates.io token tied to the publishing identity — recommend personal account first, add shiprock as co-owner after).
3. Bump version: 0.4.0 → 0.5.0 (the rebrand is a breaking change in name, so minor bump is correct under 0.x semver).
4. git tag v0.5.0, git push --tags.
5. cargo publish (no --dry-run). This is a ONE-WAY DOOR — chevron 0.5.0 cannot be unpublished, only yanked.
6. Verify on crates.io that the page renders, README shows, repository link is correct.
7. (Optional) Add shiprock as co-owner: 'cargo owner --add github:shiprock:owners chevron'.

Bonus: open a PR to nixpkgs adding pkgs/by-name/ch/chevron/package.nix that pulls from crates.io. Or defer until plxd phase 1 ships.
close_reason: Published chevron 0.5.0 to crates.io and tagged v0.5.0 (pushed). Nix flake confirmed working via 'nix run github:shiprock/chevron'. Co-owner add deferred: requires creating a 'shiprock/<team>' team on GitHub first.

title: chevrond phase 2: invalidate cache via .git/ filesystem watching
description:
Replace TTL invalidation with notify-crate watchers on the per-repo .git/ control files.

Watched set (small, bounded — avoids inotify watch-count blowup):
- .git/HEAD          (branch name, detached state)
- .git/index         (staged count)
- .git/refs/stash    (stash count)
- .git/refs/heads/*  (ahead/behind via upstream tracking)

These tiny files signal all user-visible state transitions that come from explicit git commands (commit, stage, stash, checkout, branch). Working-tree edits (modified/untracked counts) still require a libgit2 status walk on next query, with a much shorter re-cache window (e.g. 100ms).

Components (~300 LoC):
- src/daemon/watcher.rs using notify crate
- Watcher thread sends Invalidate(repo_path) messages to state thread
- Repo registration: on first STATUS query for an unwatched repo, state thread adds it to the watcher and computes RepoStatus
- Idle eviction: drop repos with no query in 1h
- Memory cap: max N watched repos (LRU eviction)

Aggressive race tests: intentionally race fs events against queries; assert no stale-data window > 100ms after a commit/stage/stash. This is the bug class that would be catastrophically worse UX than today's slow-but-correct prompt.

Blocked by phase 1.
close_reason: phase 2 complete: notify-based FS watching on each cached repo's gitdir. TTL tightened to 100ms as working-tree fallback; explicit git ops (commit/stage/checkout/stash) reflect within ~one frame via FsEvent invalidation. LRU eviction at 64 watches keeps inotify cost bounded. Tests cover event-triggered invalidation, unrelated-path immunity, path-to-workdir ancestor resolution, and LRU semantics. Manually verified: git checkout -b, git add, git commit all visible in the very next prompt without stale data.

title: chevrond phase 1: TTL-cached daemon, sync threads, text protocol, auto-spawn
description:
Build the daemon scaffolding with no filesystem watching yet. Captures ~80% of the perf win for typical interactive use because most prompt redraws happen within ~1s of the prior one.

Components (~600-800 LoC):
- src/daemon/ module, feature = 'daemon' (default-on)
- Actor model: state thread (owns HashMap<PathBuf, RepoStatus>) + listener thread (UnixListener) + 4-thread handler pool. All comms via std::sync::mpsc. NO tokio.
- Text protocol with HELLO version handshake, STATUS opcode, QUIT
- Socket at $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/plxd.sock (Linux) / /tmp/plxd-$UID/sock (macOS), mode 0700
- Auto-spawn from client on connect-fail (fork+exec self with --daemonize, double-fork+setsid, flock lockfile dedup)
- Client-side: 2ms connect timeout, 5ms total query budget, fall back to inline compute() on any failure or timeout
- 1-second TTL cache per repo path
- plx daemon {start|stop|status|logs} subcommands
- Logging to $XDG_STATE_HOME/plxd.log with simple rotation

Tests:
- Unit: state thread loop with channel-driven Query/Invalidate
- Protocol: text encode/decode round-trip
- E2E: spawn daemon as subprocess in tempdir, run plx git against tempdir-cwd, kill daemon mid-test to exercise fallback
- Invariant: inline compute() == daemon-served RepoStatus for the same fixture repo

Blocked by phase 0.
close_reason: phase 1 complete: protocol+state+paths foundations, listener+lifecycle, status_for_cwd integration across all 3 call sites, daemon serve/start/stop/status CLI, auto-spawn with setsid+flock dedup, invariant test (daemon == inline). 342 lib + 42 integration tests green; clippy clean both feature configs. Deviations: thread-per-conn instead of 4-thread pool (documented); no idle timeout, no signal handling, no file logging (small follow-ons).

title: chevrond phase 3: core.fsmonitor protocol for large monorepos
description:
Implement git's core.fsmonitor IPC so git itself queries plxd for changed paths instead of walking the full index. Targets repos in the 100k+ file range (chromium, nixpkgs, linux kernel) where libgit2 status alone is >100ms.

Most users won't need this — moderate repos (plx-sized, <10k files) are already fast enough after phase 2. So this is the last phase and entirely optional.

Components (~500 LoC + protocol study):
- Implement git fsmonitor protocol (V2 query format: client sends token + timestamp, daemon returns changed paths)
- Wire plxd to advertise itself as a fsmonitor: user sets 'git config core.fsmonitor plxd-fsmonitor' or a wrapper script
- Translate notify events into the fsmonitor change-set representation
- May require migrating to gitoxide for the daemon side if libgit2/git2's fsmonitor support is insufficient (libgit2 fsmonitor support was added in 1.5 — verify g2 0.20 exposes it)

Acceptance: 'git status' inside a 100k-file repo with plxd running drops to <20ms (vs >100ms without).

Blocked by phase 2.

title: benches harness: criterion + hyperfine for prompt segments
description: Set up an in-process criterion bench suite for the hot segments (path, git GitInfo::gather, git render, tmux_title, prompt) plus a scripts/bench.sh that runs hyperfine end-to-end against the release binary. Goal: be able to A/B perf changes (e.g. narrowing StatusOptions, folding marker-file scans) with a single command.
close_reason: Closed

title: Adaptive context-aware segment display
description:
Show prompt segments situationally based on current context rather than a fixed layout.

## Motivation
Current prompts show a fixed set of segments regardless of context. A prompt that adapts to what you are doing shows more relevant info with less noise.

## Design
- In a git repo: show git info. Outside: omit entirely (no empty segment).
- In a nix shell: show nix. Otherwise: nothing (already done).
- Long-running command just finished (>2s): show duration prominently. Otherwise: hide.
- Deep in a monorepo: show path relative to repo root, not home.
- In a Python venv: show venv name. In node project: show node version. Etc.
- Plugin/situations directory where each situation is a small check+render unit

## Key decisions
- Detection logic per-segment (like Starship) vs centralized situation resolver
- How to handle the daemon case (daemon tracks project type too?)
- Balance between smart defaults and configurability

## Acceptance criteria
- cd into a git repo shows git segment, cd out hides it (no empty space)
- Monorepo paths show repo-relative path when deep
- No segment renders empty brackets or placeholder when not relevant
- Plugin interface documented for adding new situations
notes: Follow-on filed separately for the transient-color lag bug Pat caught.
close_reason: adaptive path rendering landed. Inside a git repo, the path collapses to repo-parent-relative form (chevron src segments instead of ~ src chevron src segments), saving 2 components universally. Other adaptive criteria from the issue were verified as already-met by existing segments (git/aws/nix_shell/venv/jobs/status/cmd_duration all hide when irrelevant). Plugin architecture for new 'situations' deferred as future work. Opt-out via CHEVRON_REPO_RELATIVE_PATH=0. PathSegment does its own Repository::discover (redundant with GitSegment) — noted as a follow-on optimization that would require carrying workdir through the daemon protocol. 385 lib + 45 integration tests green.

title: Emit OSC 133 semantic terminal markers
description:
Add OSC 133 escape sequences to mark prompt boundaries so modern terminals (Ghostty, WezTerm, iTerm2, Kitty) can provide enhanced navigation.

## Motivation
OSC 133 semantic zones let terminals jump between prompts, select entire command output with one click, and correctly reflow prompts on resize. Almost no prompt tool emits these today — easy win with outsized UX impact.

## Design
- Emit OSC 133 A (prompt start) before first segment
- Emit OSC 133 B (prompt end / input start) after last segment
- Shell hooks emit OSC 133 C (input end / output start) on preexec
- Shell hooks emit OSC 133 D (output end) with exit code on precmd

## Key decisions
- Can be implemented standalone (just markers around existing output) or as part of shell integration epic
- Should detect terminal capability and skip markers on dumb terminals
- Markers are invisible — zero visual impact, pure metadata

## Acceptance criteria
- Ghostty cmd-click on prompt jumps to previous/next prompt
- iTerm2 command history navigation works
- No visual artifacts on terminals that don't support OSC 133
- Works with both Starship custom command mode and native shell hooks
close_reason: OSC 133 markers (A/B/C/D) emitted in all three shell inits. zsh wraps OSC sequences in %{...%} brackets and bash in \[...\] for prompt-width tracking; fish needs no brackets. Helper _chevron_make_prompt with REPLY keeps the three zsh PROMPT-setting sites (sync, async, transient) consistent. Default ON, opt out via CHEVRON_OSC133=0. Verified PS1 byte content via hex dump in real bash. 375 lib + 45 integration tests green.

title: Async/transient prompt via shell integration
description:
Ship shell hooks (zsh/bash/fish) that enable async prompt rendering and transient prompt collapsing.

## Motivation
Current model: Starship forks plx as a subprocess, blocks until done. Even with the daemon, there is fork overhead. Shell hooks eliminate this entirely and enable UX that subprocess-based prompts cannot do.

## Design
- Shell hooks (precmd/preexec) that query plxd or run plx inline
- Display cached/stale prompt instantly, update asynchronously when fresh data arrives
- Stale data rendered in dim color, replaced with bright when ready
- Transient prompt: on Enter, collapse previous prompt to minimal (e.g. just dollar sign) for clean scrollback and easy copy-paste
- Post-execution update: after command finishes, rewrite prompt with duration and exit code

## Key decisions
- Support zsh first (precmd/preexec), then bash (PROMPT_COMMAND), then fish
- Can coexist with Starship or replace it entirely
- Transient prompt requires terminal support for cursor movement (widely supported)

## Acceptance criteria
- Prompt appears in <5ms regardless of git state complexity
- Stale data updates within 100ms of daemon response
- Previous prompts collapse to single character in scrollback
- Works in zsh and bash
close_reason: epic complete. zsh: transient + post-exec + async stale-then-fresh. fish: transient + post-exec. bash: post-exec (transient omitted with rationale). All env-var-gated: CHEVRON_TRANSIENT, CHEVRON_TRANSIENT_DURATION_MS, CHEVRON_ASYNC. 5 child issues all closed.

title: chevrond: persistent filesystem-watching daemon
description:
Build a background daemon that maintains hot git/project state in memory, queried over Unix socket for <1ms prompt responses.

## Motivation
Every prompt tool today recomputes everything from scratch on every Enter. Powerlevel10k's gitstatusd proved a daemon architecture is 10-40x faster, but it's now unmaintained and zsh-only.

## Design
- Long-lived Rust process watching filesystem via macOS FSEvents / Linux inotify
- Integrates with git core.fsmonitor for efficient dirty checking
- Maintains repo state (branch, staged, modified, untracked, stash, ahead/behind) always warm
- Serves queries over Unix socket
- Graceful fallback: if daemon not running, compute inline like today

## Key decisions
- Use gitoxide instead of git2 for the daemon (better async support)
- Socket protocol: simple binary or JSON request/response
- Lifecycle: auto-start on first prompt query, idle timeout shutdown
- Multi-repo support: track multiple repos simultaneously

## Acceptance criteria
- plx git queries daemon when available, falls back to direct git2
- Prompt latency <5ms with daemon vs current 50-500ms
- Daemon reacts to filesystem changes within 100ms
- Works on macOS and Linux
design:
## Architectural primitive: separate compute from render

Pull the data-computation half of `segments::git::render` into a pure
`RepoStatus` struct + `compute(repo: &Repository) -> RepoStatus` function.
`render(status: &RepoStatus, from_bg) -> String` becomes pure formatting.

The daemon becomes a cache over `compute`. Both inline-fallback and daemon
paths call identical `compute` code — daemon is an optimization, never a
dependency. This invariant is enforced by a test: inline-compute vs
daemon-served must produce identical RepoStatus for identical state.

`GitInfo::gather` is the prototype — it already does this split for the
tmux-title path. Generalize.

## Concurrency: actor model, NO async runtime

- Watcher thread (notify crate) → Invalidate(path) messages
- State thread owns HashMap<PathBuf, RepoStatus>, processes serially
- Listener thread (UnixListener) + 4-thread handler pool
- All inter-thread comms via std::sync::mpsc

Why sync/no tokio: prompt workloads are low-QPS, tokio's startup cost is
non-trivial, and the actor model gives clean panic isolation + trivial
unit tests (drive state thread by channel messages, no I/O needed).

## Protocol: line-oriented text over UDS

Socket: $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/plxd.sock (Linux) or /tmp/plxd-$UID/sock (macOS),
mode 0700.

```
> HELLO 1
< HELLO 1
> STATUS /path
< OK branch=... staged=N modified=N untracked=N ...
> QUIT
```

Why text: debuggable with `socat`, trivial protocol code, schema evolves
by adding keys. Parse cost is <1µs vs ~200µs socket round-trip.

Stateless beyond HELLO. No streams, no subscriptions.

## Lifecycle: client auto-spawns, never depends

- Client connect with 2ms timeout, query with 5ms total budget
- On connect-fail: fall back to inline `compute` AND fork+exec self as
  daemon (don't wait for it; next render uses the warm daemon)
- flock on lockfile to dedup simultaneous spawns (12 shells → 1 daemon)
- Double-fork + setsid for detachment

## Module layout

```
src/
  repo_status_data.rs  # NEW: RepoStatus struct + compute()
  segments/git.rs       # pure render() over RepoStatus
  daemon/               # feature = "daemon", default-on
    mod.rs proto.rs state.rs watcher.rs listener.rs
    lifecycle.rs client.rs
```

Client mode never executes daemon code paths. `--no-default-features
--features=core,banner,weather` produces a daemon-less embedded build.

## Anti-patterns to refuse

- Arc<Mutex<HashMap>> shared across threads (use channels)
- Loading TOML config in the daemon (render lives in client only)
- Streaming/subscription opcodes (stateless request/response only)
- Shelling out to `git` from the daemon (libgit2/gitoxide only)
- Persisting cache across daemon restarts (in-memory only)
- Async I/O for the socket (sync threads are simpler at this scale)
- Letting client-side fallback drift from daemon output (invariant test)

## Phased rollout

Children: phase 0 → phase 1 → phase 2 → phase 3, sequential.

Phase 0 is independently valuable (cleaner tests) and unblocks all later
phases. Phase 1 captures ~80% of the perf win with TTL caching and no FS
watcher. Each phase is strictly additive — earlier phase's code keeps
working if a later phase is disabled.

title: E2e: live prompt survives a daemon restart (reconnect)
description: Daemon-backed e2e proving chevron-1mh end to end: with CHEVRON_LIVE=1, stop+restart chevrond and confirm the prompt still redraws on a post-restart event (the helper reconnected). The reconnect policy + backoff are already unit-tested in src/subscribe.rs (next_action/backoff_delay); this covers the wiring. Extend the ffu.1 harness; likely #[ignore]'d (restart + notify timing) and shaken out in CI. Cleanest path: subprocess daemon (chevron daemon start/stop) so it can be killed, re-register the repo watch after restart via a status query, then assert redraw.

title: daemon test flaky under parallel runner: try_publish_event_persists_cmd_start_and_end
description: Failed once during a full parallel cargo test run (2026-06-10 session), passes 3/3 in isolation and survived the 5x pre-push stress runs afterwards. Likely shared-state or timing sensitivity between daemon client tests under the parallel test runner. Treat a CI failure here as signal per the repo's flake policy; investigate serialization or per-test socket isolation if it recurs.
close_reason: Root cause found by reading the dispatch path: the test's flush barrier (STATUS for the socket tempdir) never reached the state actor -- handle_status short-circuits non-repo paths in the LISTENER -- so the direct SQLite read could race the actor's async commit under load. Replaced with a deadline poll (10s, 10ms steps) on the row WHERE finished_at IS NOT NULL, which also waits out the CmdStart-only intermediate state. Each query_row is its own WAL read transaction, so every retry sees a fresh snapshot.

title: collapse transient on interrupted/EOF'd lines (zsh)
description: From the survey (chevron-f6g): p10k maps send-break and ^D to its zle-line-finish handler so aborted lines ALSO collapse in scrollback; olets/zsh-transient-prompt installs a TRAPINT that renders the transient on ^C; oh-my-posh detects KEYS == \\4 (^D) for EOF. chevron only collapses via the accept-line override, so ^C-aborted lines, ^D exits, and the final pre-exit prompt stay full-width in scrollback. Polish, not a glitch (the rewrite is now gated on real collapses). Consider the zle-line-finish approach, which unifies all line-ending paths instead of enumerating widgets.

title: chevron configure v2: shell-rc install, icon swapping, two-line preset
description:
Follow-up to chevron-y4x (v1 shipped). The three deferred items from v1's scope.

## Scope

**--install-shell flag**: after writing config.toml, optionally append the shell snippet to ~/.zshrc / ~/.bashrc / ~/.config/fish/config.fish. Must:
- Detect existing `chevron init` lines and skip if present
- Backup the rc file to <rc>.bak before modifying
- Show a unified diff and confirm before writing
- Atomic: tmp + rename like writer.rs

**Per-segment icon swapping**: honor `[icons] mode = "nerd|unicode|ascii"` from config. Currently v1 stores the preference but segments still emit hard-coded glyphs. Touch the segments that have a glyph (git BRANCH_ICON, status, character, jobs) to consult a central icons module. ~150 LoC across segments.

**Two-line prompt preset**: add a `linebreak` segment (emits `\n` plus a fresh background reset) and a `Preset::TwoLine` that uses it. Wizard gains a 6th preset.

## Acceptance

- --install-shell appends without duplicating
- ASCII icon mode renders branch as `*` not Nerd Font glyph
- Two-line preset renders path on line 1, character on line 2 in a real terminal

## Effort

~400 LoC + tests across segments and the configure module.

title: chevron doctor v2: perf benchmark + daemon round-trip + config dump
description:
Follow-up to chevron-c65 (v1 shipped). Adds the heavier checks deferred from v1 to keep that PR small.

## Scope

**Perf benchmark** — 5 runs each of `chevron path`, `chevron git`, `chevron prompt` in the current directory. Report median + p95 latency. Warn if `prompt` > 50ms, critical if > 200ms. Suggest CHEVRON_ASYNC=1 on warn.

**Async cache health** — if CHEVRON_CACHE_FILE set, verify writable + first-line PWD matches current dir. Detect stale cache for wrong directory.

**Daemon round-trip** — use `daemon::client::try_version`, distinguish: socket missing / stale (no listener) / version-drift (CLI != daemon) / OK. Currently doctor reports only build-time `cfg!(feature = "daemon")`.

**commands.db check** — open the db read-only, verify schema_version matches CLI's expected, report row count and file size. Reuse `history::check_schema` if present.

**outputs/ dir check** — file count + total size + oldest entry timestamp. Warn if size > 500MB (capture data accumulating).

**Config dump** — parse Config via `config::Config::load`, print effective values. Add `--no-secrets` flag that masks any field whose key matches /token|secret|key|password/i.

## Acceptance

- All five new sections render in default text + --json
- --no-secrets actually masks (test with a known secret-shaped key)
- Daemon round-trip distinguishes the four states above
- Benchmark warn/critical thresholds wired through

## Estimate

~300 LoC + tests. Larger pieces are perf timing harness and daemon round-trip error mapping.

title: Transient color-correction rewrite races with CHEVRON_LIVE / CHEVRON_ASYNC redraw paths
description:
## Symptom

With CHEVRON_LIVE=1 + CHEVRON_ASYNC=1 + CHEVRON_HISTORY=1 all enabled simultaneously (full daily-drive config from dotfiles 2026-05-24), the transient prompt rewrite occasionally appears as a DUPLICATE line rather than overwriting the original transient.

User-visible:

```
❯ echo "test"           ← gray transient (from accept-line widget)
❯ echo "test"           ← green color-corrected (from precmd DSR rewrite)
test                    ← command output
```

Expected (and observed historically with just CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=1 on):

```
❯ echo "test"           ← color-corrected in place (green if exit 0)
test                    ← command output
```

## Suspected cause

The transient rewrite (src/shell.rs zsh init `_chevron_precmd`'s color-correction block) uses DSR + absolute positioning to overwrite the transient line. It captures `R_saved` in preexec and `R_current` in precmd. If something else moves the cursor or triggers a redraw between those points, the math is off.

Two new redraw paths landed in chevron-1yn that could interfere when enabled together:

- Phase 3 (CHEVRON_LIVE=1): `_chevron_live_callback` reads an EVENT line from `chevron subscribe`, calls `_chevron_start_async` which fires `zle reset-prompt` ~5-50ms later.
- chevron-7fs.5 (CHEVRON_ASYNC=1): `_chevron_async_callback` also calls `zle reset-prompt` when a background `chevron prompt` invocation lands.

Race window: between preexec's DSR query (R_saved) and precmd's DSR query (R_current). A `zle reset-prompt` in this window would redraw the prompt (moving the cursor) without the transient code being aware.

## Repro

Single-keystroke command in a shell with the full config:

```
$ CHEVRON_LIVE=1 CHEVRON_ASYNC=1 CHEVRON_HISTORY=1 exec zsh
$ echo "test"
```

Observed: 1-of-3 trials shows the duplicate. Race is timing-dependent.

## Triage steps not yet completed

Need to isolate which combination produces the bug by disabling each axis:

1. `CHEVRON_LIVE=0 CHEVRON_ASYNC=1` — does ASYNC alone cause it?
2. `CHEVRON_LIVE=1 CHEVRON_ASYNC=0` — does LIVE alone cause it?
3. `CHEVRON_LIVE=0 CHEVRON_ASYNC=0` — does it persist without either?

If (3) shows the bug, it's something else (perhaps `chevron event cmd-start`/`cmd-end` forks moving the cursor).

If (1) or (2) shows it, that path is the conflict.

## Hypothesis (pre-triage)

The Phase 1 cmd-start/cmd-end forks (3-15ms each for the subprocess hop) could be advancing the cursor row that the DSR query later captures. The fork itself doesn't write to the terminal — but the subprocess's stderr might if the daemon isn't responding cleanly.

Alternative: the `zle reset-prompt` from async-render lands AFTER precmd's color-correction has run, redrawing the prompt below the just-rewritten transient line. Result: the rewrite is buried, and the user sees the original gray + the newly-drawn fresh prompt.

## Workaround for users today

Set `CHEVRON_TRANSIENT=0` to disable the transient prompt entirely. Loses the collapse-on-Enter feature but eliminates the rewrite race. Set `CHEVRON_LIVE=0` and/or `CHEVRON_ASYNC=0` to disable individual fast-paths if those are the culprits.

## Files to look at

- src/shell.rs zsh init: the `_chevron_preexec` DSR capture (line ~21-30) and `_chevron_precmd` rewrite block (line ~81-129)
- The async callback path (`_chevron_async_callback`, line ~230)
- The live callback path (`_chevron_live_callback`, line ~248)

## Reference

The transient rewrite already has a documented limitation block (in `_chevron_precmd`, line ~73-105) covering scroll detection and skip conditions. This issue extends that list with "redraw races" as a new failure mode the existing guards don't catch.

Probably the fix is either:
- A re-entrancy guard around the color-correction (skip if another redraw is in flight)
- Use atomic in-place rewrite that doesn't depend on absolute positioning (some kind of "delete-and-prepend" widget operation if zsh has one — unclear if it does)
- Defer color-correction until AFTER the next prompt is drawn (using zle reset-prompt's deferred mode if such a thing exists)

## Priority

P2. Reproducible visual regression in the full daily-drive config. The dotfiles 2026-05-24 update enabled all three opt-ins together for the first time; this race didn't surface in pre-1yn testing because Phase 1 hooks and Phase 3 subscribe didn't exist yet.
notes:
## Update 2026-05-24

The double-prompt-line behaviour was originally observed running an
INTERMEDIATE chevron binary (Phase 1-era: had `event` subcommand but
NOT `history`/`capture`/`subscribe`). After updating dotfiles
flake.lock to current unstable HEAD (chevron commit c64a7d9, with
all Phase 1-4 + VERSION-check), the bug does NOT reproduce on
initial testing in a fresh shell.

This downgrades the priority but doesn't close the issue — race
conditions are intermittent by definition. Possible explanations:

1. The bug genuinely existed only in the intermediate Phase 1-era
   binary and was fixed in later commits (Phase 3's stash of
   _chevron_last_exit / _chevron_last_duration, or some shell.rs
   change between 12c88ea and c64a7d9).
2. The race is timing-dependent and just didn't fire in the first
   few test commands on the new binary.
3. The race depended on the daemon being a specific (older)
   version too; the daemon now matches the CLI and the timing
   shifted out of the race window.

## Recommended action

Keep open at P3 (down from P2). If the user re-encounters the
double-prompt over a week of daily-drive, escalate back to P2 with
the new repro. If it's still not seen after a week, close as
"resolved by the chevron binary version-pinning fix that landed
2026-05-24."

## Original CHEVRON_LIVE hypothesis was wrong

The original triage assumed CHEVRON_LIVE=1 + CHEVRON_ASYNC=1 were
racing. But the user's intermediate binary didn't even have the
Phase 3 `subscribe` subcommand — so CHEVRON_LIVE=1 was a no-op
env var that the OLD init script ignored. The actual race (if it
re-occurs) is between CHEVRON_ASYNC's zle reset-prompt path and
either Phase 1's `chevron event cmd-start` fork OR the transient
color-correction's DSR positioning.

## Next steps if re-occurs

Repro steps would be: `CHEVRON_ASYNC=0 exec zsh; echo test; echo
test`. If clean, async is the culprit. If still doubled,
investigate the Phase 1 preexec fork's effect on cursor row.
close_reason: Hardened: the generation guard provably cannot catch a refresh landing between accept-line and the next precmd (gen only bumps in precmd), so _chevron_async_callback now drops results while _chevron_transient_collapsed is set -- the result describes a finished cycle and the next precmd re-renders anyway. Covers both ASYNC and LIVE (live funnels through the same callback). The async axis was swept in-harness with a new 16-cycle Enter-timing test around the refresh arrival (async_refresh_at_accept_never_duplicates, 48 cycles across 3 runs): the duplicate did NOT reproduce on the current build, so the original 1-of-3 repro (2026-05-24, pre-dating the collapse-flag gating, PS2 guard, stderr fix, and DSR linger) may already have been fixed by those. The LIVE axis remains untestable in-harness (needs daemon; known gap). Re-test the daily-drive config on >=this build; reopen with a fresh repro if it persists.

title: Spike: output diff for recurring commands
description:
Spike issue. Run `git status` 10 times today, chevron has all 10 captures (Phase 4). `chevron history diff --cmd 'git status' --last 2` shows what changed between consecutive runs. Novel because nobody else captures output by default.

## Why this is frontier

- atuin doesn't capture output → can't do this
- mcfly doesn't capture output → can't do this
- No comparable tool captures by default; chevron's per-cwd opt-in capture is the unlock

Real use cases:
- "Did `kubectl get pods` change since last check?" → diff shows pod status delta
- "Did `cargo build` add warnings overnight?" → diff shows warning count drift
- "Did `find . -name '*.tmp'` find new temp files?" → diff shows new entries

## Spike deliverables

1. Diff format: textual diff, semantic diff (e.g. for JSON output), or both?
2. UX: `chevron history diff` subcommand? Or `chevron diff --cmd ... --between N..M`? Or interactive in chevron-xkz (TUI)?
3. Performance: diffing two 10MB captures should be sub-second. Use similar crate or hand-rolled? Plain text vs ANSI-stripped diff?
4. Edge cases: commands with random IDs in output (timestamps, UUIDs); commands whose output is just a count that changed (`wc -l`); commands whose output is truncated.

## Output

Design doc + decision. Implementation estimate.

## Effort

Spike: S-M (~2-3 days). Implementation: M (~600 LoC including diff engine integration).

## Synergy

- Builds entirely on chevron-1yn.4 (capture) — already shipped
- TUI integration (chevron-xkz): preview pane could show diff-vs-previous-run for the highlighted cmd
- Reuses ANSI-stripping logic from `chevron history --grep-output`

## Out of scope

- Live diff (auto-diff every run as it happens). v1 is on-demand.
- Cross-machine diff (Phase 8 sync dependency).
- Multi-stream diff (separate stdout vs stderr). v1 diffs the combined captured byte stream.

## Filed as spike

The UX design is the hard part (textual diff feels primitive for a "smart" feature; semantic diff is hard to generalize). Spike first.

title: Spike: federated sync via user-owned git repo (alternative to chevron-wvn atuin-compat)
description:
Spike issue. Alternative to the atuin-compat-vs-chevron-native decision in chevron-wvn: sync chevrond's commands.db via the user's OWN git repo. E2EE at rest via git's standard tooling (gpg, age-keyed). No chevron-server infrastructure required.

## Why this is frontier

Nobody syncs shell history via the user's own git repo. atuin uses a dedicated server. dotfiles tools (chezmoi, yadm) sync configs but not databases. The chevron-native angle: developers already trust git for cross-machine sync; making history a normal git artifact is dead-simple operationally.

Aligns with chevron's local-first ethos: no server to host, no central account, no recurring trust.

## Spike deliverables

1. SQLite + git: can a binary SQLite file merge cleanly via git? (Probably not — binary merges are painful.) Alternative: export to JSONL on commit, import on pull. Schema-aware merge.
2. CRDT-ish conflict resolution: ULIDs are sortable so "all rows from all machines, deduplicated by id" is the merge rule. Soft-delete via tombstone rows. Same approach atuin uses internally.
3. UX: `chevron sync push` / `chevron sync pull`? Or daemon auto-syncs on idle?
4. Encryption: at rest the git repo can be on github.com (encrypted blobs only) or s3-with-server-side-crypto. Per-record E2EE via age (https://age-encryption.org) is well-trodden.
5. Compaction: history grows unboundedly. Need a "snapshot" mechanism analogous to git's pack files.

## Output

Design doc comparing this to chevron-wvn's atuin-compat path. Decision either way (or "do both, let users pick").

## Effort

Spike: M-L (~1-2 weeks of design + proof-of-concept). Implementation: L. Significantly LESS than running a server (chevron-wvn's atuin-compat path requires server-side infra).

## Relationship to chevron-wvn

chevron-wvn is the broader "design sync" spike. This issue is one specific PATH chevron-wvn might choose. Could potentially supersede or merge with chevron-wvn; coordinate.

## Synergy with local-first ethos

Most chevron users already use git for dotfiles. Adding history sync via git means zero new infrastructure to learn, zero new accounts, full transparency (history is just files in a repo).

## Out of scope for spike

- Compatibility with non-git VCSes (Fossil, Pijul). Spike focuses on git.
- Mobile clients. Not a chevron concern.
- Web UI. Not a chevron concern.

## Filed as spike

The big unknown is whether SQLite-via-git is operationally sane. Spike answers that question.

title: Spike: time-travel scrollback prompt redraw
description:
Spike issue. Scroll past an old prompt in your terminal scrollback, hit a key (Alt-T?), chevron redraws the prompt with git state AS IT WAS at that moment. Genuinely novel "shell-aware session brain" UX.

## Why this is hard

- Detect scrollback position via DSR (we've fought this before for the transient-color rewrite — see chevron-4xq history)
- Find the most-recent OSC 133 A marker above the cursor → that's the prompt we want to redraw
- Look up the historical commands.head_sha for that prompt
- Render a fresh prompt with that historical git state (requires git2's reset-cached-state mechanic or a separate `chevron history-prompt <head_sha>` rendering path)
- Print the redrawn prompt in-place WITHOUT scrolling

## Spike deliverables

1. Feasibility check: can we reliably find the prompt boundary above the scrollback cursor via DSR + OSC 133? (Likely yes if user has the markers; degrade gracefully otherwise.)
2. Rendering path: how does chevron render a prompt for a historical git state without checking out that SHA? libgit2 can read past commits cheaply — but RepoStatus's compute() looks at the CURRENT index/HEAD. Need to either parameterise the compute path or fork a temp "snapshot" Repository.
3. UX: what key? what visual signal that the prompt is "frozen in time"? does subsequent typing redraw with current state?
4. Edge cases: branches that no longer exist (deleted feature branches); SHAs that got GC'd; stash that was popped.

## Output

Design doc + decision filed as bd decision-type issue. Estimate to implement (or "this is more complex than expected, defer").

## Effort

Spike: M (~3-5 days of design + prototyping, no production code).
Implementation if greenlit: M-L depending on edge-case handling.

## Synergy

- Requires branch + commit context on commands (chevron-1ys.1.1) — without head_sha per command this can't work at all
- Reuses OSC 133 markers shipped in chevron-1yn (Phase 1)
- DSR query work from the transient-color rewrite is reusable

## Filed as spike not feature

Could be 2 weeks or 6 months depending on how scrollback-aware terminals are willing to play ball. Spike first, decide after.

title: Cross-pane command awareness in tmux
description:
Pane 1's failed cargo build shows up as a status chip in pane 2's prompt. Builds entirely on Phase 3 (subscribe) infrastructure + tmux pane id env vars.

## Why this is frontier

Nobody else can do this:
- atuin has cross-machine sync but no cross-pane awareness within ONE machine
- p10k/Starship are stateless per-pane renderers
- Warp doesn't even use tmux's pane model
- Requires both (a) cross-pane data sharing and (b) live push to a different shell, which is precisely what chevrond + chevron subscribe ship

## Surface

- Shell init stashes \$TMUX_PANE in a chevron env var (CHEVRON_PANE_ID)
- CmdStart payload includes pane_id (new optional kv on the wire, forward-compat)
- chevrond's broadcast emits cross-pane events: when pane X's command finishes with non-zero exit, all OTHER panes in the same tmux session get a pane_id-filtered event
- Shell init (in subscribed mode) registers a sibling-pane indicator: receives "pane X failed `cmd`" event, displays a chip in this pane's prompt

## Acceptance criteria

- Pane 1 runs `cargo build` that fails with exit 1
- Within ~200ms, pane 2's next prompt redraw shows a small chip: "⚠ pane 1 failed `cargo build`"
- The chip clears when pane 2 starts a new command (or after configurable timeout)
- Outside tmux (no \$TMUX_PANE), no chip is shown
- Disabled by default; opt-in via CHEVRON_CROSS_PANE=1

## Effort

M (~350 LoC across shell init + wire schema bump + daemon broadcast logic + tests).

## Synergy

- Builds on chevron-1yn.3 (Phase 3 subscribe) — exact mechanism already exists
- Wire-protocol forward-compat means existing daemons silently ignore the pane_id field; new clients consume it
- `chevron history --pane <id>` becomes a side-effect free filter (file as small follow-up)

## Out of scope

- Cross-pane awareness without tmux (different terminal multiplexers). zellij has pane env vars too; could land as P4 follow-up.
- Cross-MACHINE pane awareness (would require sync infrastructure). Phase 8.
- Bidirectional command control (pane 2 cancels pane 1's command). Display only.

## Use cases

- Long-running build in pane 1; pane 2 sees it fail and updates immediately
- `kubectl logs -f` in pane 1 starts spitting errors; pane 2 sees the error count tick up
- A grep over a huge dir running in pane 1; pane 2 prompt shows "still running 12s..." indicator

The shell becomes a multi-pane workspace where ALL panes know what every OTHER pane is doing. Atuin can't do this. p10k can't do this. Warp arguably can but only in their proprietary terminal.

title: Tier 2: Design spikes (time-travel, output diff, federated sync)
description:
Three frontier features that are NOVEL but require design work before implementation. File as spikes — produce a design doc + decision before committing to implementation.

## Children

- Time-travel scrollback (spike) — scroll past an old prompt, hit a key, see git state as it was then. Listed in chevron-1yn epic notes as a "wild idea."
- Output diff for recurring commands (spike) — `git status` from 5 minutes ago vs now. Sliding window over captured output.
- Federated sync via user's own git repo (spike) — alternative to chevron-wvn's atuin-compat path. E2EE at rest via git's own crypto, no chevron-server infrastructure required.

## Why spikes, not features

Each has a "could be 2 weeks or could be 6 months" cone of uncertainty. The spike output (design doc + scope estimate) lets you decide whether to invest. Without a spike, you can't tell.

## Sequence

Order doesn't matter — independent.

title: Command-template simplification (canonical cmd form for ranking)
description:
Normalize commands to a canonical "template" form so that `git commit -m "fix bug A"` and `git commit -m "fix bug B"` count as the same command for frequency and dedup purposes.

## Why this matters

Today, every literal cmd string is distinct in the commands table. That breaks:
- Frequency-based ranking (chevron-dfd): "you ran `git commit -m "..."` 50 times" gets split across 50 distinct rows
- Dedup (chevron-haa): two `git commit -m` invocations with different messages won't dedup
- Future learned ranking: template-overlap features become useful only with normalization

mcfly's `SimplifiedCommand` (src/simplified_command.rs in cantino/mcfly) is the reference: collapses quoted strings to `QUOTED`, file paths to `PATH`, numeric literals to `N`, etc. Same approach as autocomplete tools (Z, fasd).

## Surface

- New column on commands table: `cmd_template` (TEXT, computed at write time)
- New module `src/templater.rs` with `simplify(cmd: &str) -> String`
- Simplification rules (initial set):
  - Single-quoted strings → `'QUOTED'`
  - Double-quoted strings → `"QUOTED"`
  - Heredocs → `HEREDOC`
  - File paths (`./...`, `~/...`, `/...`) → `PATH`
  - Numeric literals → `N`
  - URLs → `URL`
  - Pipes / redirections preserved (structural)
  - Flag values not simplified (`-m FOO` keeps `-m` but FOO depends on its shape)

## Acceptance criteria

- `simplify("git commit -m \"fix bug A\"")` == `simplify("git commit -m \"fix bug B\"")` (both → `git commit -m "QUOTED"`)
- `simplify("cd ~/src/chevron")` → `cd PATH`
- `simplify("curl https://example.com")` → `curl URL`
- Round-trip: writing simplified to a new column doesn't change the original `cmd` value
- Schema migration v3 → v4 (or v2 → v3 if click-feedback hasn't landed yet — coordinate with that issue)
- Snapshot tests for at least 20 representative command shapes (git, docker, kubectl, ssh, find, grep, etc.)

## Effort

S (~250 LoC + ~50 snapshot fixtures + tests).

## Sequence

After chevron-dfd (frecency) — frecency can use simplified cmd as the grouping key. Could ship before, but chevron-dfd is the natural caller.

Could be done at the same time as chevron-haa (dedup) — dedup is the other obvious caller.

## Synergy

`cmd_template` is computed once at write time (in chevrond's CmdEnd handler), stored alongside cmd. No re-computation on read. Ranking and dedup just GROUP BY cmd_template.

## Reference

mcfly's `src/simplified_command.rs`. About 200 LoC of regex-based normalization. Worth borrowing the regex set as a starting point.

## Out of scope

- AST-level parsing (would need a shell-script parser). Regex normalization is the well-trodden cheap path.
- Per-language templating (Python vs Bash vs SQL). One generic templater.
- User-configurable simplification rules. v1 ships fixed rules; revisit if users complain.

title: Phase 8 sync: design spike — atuin-compat vs chevron-native wire format
description:
The chevron-1yn epic notes pin Atuin-style E2EE sync as Phase 8+ work. Schema is already designed for it (ULIDs, ms timestamps, hostname + session_id first-class, output in separate file). This issue is the DESIGN SPIKE that decides the wire format before any implementation lands.

## The decision

Two paths:

### Path A: Atuin-compatible

Implement atuin's wire protocol + record encryption. Users can push to existing atuin servers (self-hosted or atuin.sh hosted) AND keep chevron's local-first daemon.

Pros:
- Leverages atuin's existing server ecosystem
- Users can sync history between chevron and atuin shells (mixed-tool households)
- Server-side hosting infrastructure already exists (paid hosted + self-hosted free)

Cons:
- atuin's record format doesn't model branch state, repo identity, or output capture — we'd lose those fields on round-trip
- Tied to atuin's roadmap (their protocol versioning)
- Less control over conflict resolution semantics

### Path B: Chevron-native

Own wire protocol + record format. Sync compatible only with chevron servers.

Pros:
- Native modeling of chevron-only fields (output_bytes, output_truncated, branch state if we add it later)
- Full control over crypto suite, conflict resolution, server protocol
- Can design for chevron-specific patterns (e.g. branch + command DAG)

Cons:
- Significant server work (build, maintain, optionally host)
- Users in mixed atuin+chevron environments can't sync between them
- New ecosystem to bootstrap from zero

## Spike deliverables

1. Concrete schema diff: what gets sent over the wire under each path? Which chevron columns drop on atuin-compat?
2. Crypto suite proposal: atuin uses paserk-ish ed25519 + xchacha20. Path A inherits; Path B picks (recommend same suite).
3. Conflict resolution model: timestamps + tombstones? CRDT? Last-write-wins per ULID?
4. Server architecture: what does the server need to do? Storage backend (SQLite, Postgres, S3-compatible)?
5. Migration path: existing local-only chevron users — what does opt-in to sync look like?
6. Decision document filed as bd decision-type issue with rationale.

## Why a spike (not a feature)

We don't have an active need yet. The schema is sync-ready, the code isn't. A spike forces the decision before infrastructure work compounds in either direction.

## Sequence

P3. File now because the decision should be made BEFORE chevron-haa (history lifecycle delete/prune) lands — once we have hard-deletes in master, retrofitting tombstones for sync is painful.

## Out of scope for the spike

- Actual implementation. Spike outputs a design doc + decision; implementation is a future epic.
- Server hosting infrastructure. If we pick Path B, server work is a sibling epic.
- Cross-machine sync UX (Ctrl-R'ing through history from another machine). Same — that's the visible payoff but it requires the infrastructure first.

## Reference

- chevron-1yn epic notes (closed): see "Sync-readiness pins" section
- atuin's sync docs: https://docs.atuin.sh/cli/guide/sync/
- atuin's record format: docs.atuin.sh + the records v2 RFC in atuinsh/atuin

title: Recording-side filters: history_filter, cwd_filter, store_failed
description:
Three write-time exclusion controls. Drop matching commands at CmdEnd time so they never enter the commands table. Distinct from query-time --exclude flags (which filter at read time but the rows are still recorded).

## Why this matters

Users want "never record commands matching these patterns" rules. Leading-space (HISTCONTROL=ignorespace) is too fragile — it requires per-invocation discipline. A persistent rule that says 'never record aws sso login *' or 'never record anything from ~/Downloads' is the canonical fix. atuin pairs this with history prune --dry-run for retroactive cleanup of pre-rule entries.

## Config surface (new [history] section)

[history]
history_filter = ["^aws sso login", "^secret-tool"]  # regexes; cmd matching ANY is dropped
cwd_filter = ["~/Downloads/.*", ".*/tmp$"]  # regexes; cwd matching ANY drops the entry
store_failed = true  # default true (matches today); false drops non-zero exits

## Acceptance criteria

- A command matching a history_filter regex doesn't appear in 'chevron history'
- A command run in a cwd matching cwd_filter doesn't appear
- store_failed = false drops rows where exit_status != 0 (kept silent: NULL exits — still-running — are kept until they finish)
- Snapshot-tested with fixture filter configs

## Effort

S+S+XS combined, total ~250 LoC + tests. File as one issue because all three share insertion point (chevrond's CmdEnd / state.rs).

## Synergy with chevron-ckz

Secret detection (chevron-ckz, filed) is the third leg of the same stool: instead of regex rules, ckz inspects content for known secret patterns. Likely shares plumbing once both land.

## Out of scope

- Per-shell or per-session filter rules. Global config only for v1.
- Allow-lists (the inverse: 'only record commands matching X'). Drop into 'out of scope unless requested.'

title: Frecency scoring for command ranking
description:
Rank search results by a configurable combination of frequency (how often run) and recency (how recently). atuin's frecency_score_multiplier / frequency_score_multiplier / recency_score_multiplier model.

## Why this matters

Users notice "the right command at the top" more than they notice fuzzy vs prefix matching. atuin's frecency-default ranking is what makes its TUI feel smart. Without this, chevron's TUI ships with chronological-default ranking which works but feels dumber.

## Surface

- New 'rank' column on commands table (or computed on-the-fly initially; could become persistent later)
- New [search.ranking] config section:
  - frequency_weight (default: 1.0)
  - recency_weight (default: 1.0)
  - frecency_weight (default: 0.0 — opt-in to combined frecency)
- 'chevron history --rank frecency|recent|alpha|duration' to override
- TUI uses the configured default

## Acceptance criteria

- A heavily-used command ('git status') outranks a rarely-used one in default frecency view
- A recently-used command outranks an old one with equal frequency
- 'chevron history --rank alpha' returns alphabetically-sorted results (current behaviour is started_at-DESC; this changes default to frecency only when configured)
- Snapshot-tested with fixture commands of varying frequency + recency

## Effort

M (~300 LoC + tests).

## Sequence

Best after the TUI lands (chevron-1yq.1.1). Ranking is invisible without an interactive consumer that surfaces it.

## Synergy

The actor's existing CmdEnd handler is the place to increment a 'last_run / run_count' pair on the matching row (or recompute frecency on every render — pick based on benchmarks).

## Out of scope

- Per-cwd frecency (rank by 'how often used IN THIS CWD'). Useful but distinct algorithm.
- Hand-tunable scoring formulas beyond multipliers.

title: History lifecycle commands: delete --query, dedup, prune
description:
